value.Title: Solar foundations manufacturer American Steel and Aluminum expands operations

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHJ-YK53-SGR6-C2M7-00000-00Solar foundations manufacturer American Steel and Aluminum expands operations2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisSolar foundations manufacturer American Steel and Aluminum expands operations

Contract manufacturer American Steel and Aluminum (ASA) has opened a new 50,000 sq-ft facility in Syracuse, New York, to meet growing demand from markets including renewable energy.

The interior of the American Steel and Aluminums new facility in Syracuse, New York. Credit: American Steel and Aluminum

This is ASAs second facility in Syracuse. The company hopes to shorten product lead times and introduce new processing capabilities in this new plant.

This expansion is a direct response to what were hearing from the market, said Sam Blatchford, president of ASA. Our customers in data centers, defense and energy are scaling fast, and they need a domestic manufacturer who can scale with them. Thats exactly what were building in Syracuse.

ASA entered the solar foundations market in February with its ground screw solution. Ground screws are designed to embed in less-than-ideal soil conditions, such as sites with glacial till, cobble or bedrock. ASA is vertically integrated and handles production from raw domestic steel through final product. This new facility has made additional space for for processing, fabrication and fulfillment.

ASA now operates more than 450,000 sq ft of manufacturing and processing space across facilities in Maine, Massachusetts, New York and New Hampshire. The companys Syracuse locations are hubs for steel processing and distribution.

value.Title: National Grid to Expand DLR Technology to 585km of British Grid Transmission Routes

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-D2H3-S47X-F0Y6-00000-00National Grid to Expand DLR Technology to 585km of British Grid Transmission Routes2026-05-02T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisNational Grid to Expand DLR Technology to 585km of British Grid Transmission Routes

The DLR system involves the deployment of sensors that continuously monitor the function of electricity transmission lines, in order to calculate a dynamic line rating, from which the system takes its name.

National Grid will expand its Dynamic Line Rating (DLR) technology to 585km of existing transmission routes, which it expects will save consumers up to 50 million in constraint costs over the next five years.

The DLR system involves the deployment of sensors that continuously monitor the function of electricity transmission lines, in order to calculate a dynamic line rating, from which the system takes its name. This rating includes variables such as the wire specifications and weather conditions to generate a figure that represents the maximum safe power carrying capacity of a wire, which is often lower, and much more accurate, than conventional assumptions made without the use of continuous monitoring.

National Grid estimates that the use of DLR technology allows transmission lines to carry an average of 8% more electricity, without posing an additional risk, and first deployed the technology on a trial basis along its networks in the US states of New York and Massachusetts, in tandem with US-based DLR provider LineVision. National Grid then brought the technology to the UK through a two-year trial programme in Cumbria beginning in 2022 and expanded the DLR system last year, adding 275km of new transmission routes.

The grid operator estimates that its use of DLR technology in the last five years has saved 21 million by removing the need for constraint costs to be paid.

The next generation of DLR technology will be rolled out over the next five years, through a contract with LineVision, Belgian grid monitoring firm Ampacimon and Danish cybersecurity company Heimdall. National Grid expects the majority of upgrades to be completed by 2028, and expects work to include the use of drones to mount sensors to live power lines.

By embracing innovation and using cutting-edge technology such as DLR we are unlocking greater capacity on our existing network and enabling more renewable energy to flow to homes and businesses, said National Grid Electricity Transmission president Alice Delahunty.

Integrating grid-enhancing technologies such as these will deliver significant savings for consumers by reducing constraint costs and is an important part of our work to upgrade the grid to deliver a resilient, future-ready energy system."

The expansion of Britains operational grid infrastructure would be a significant benefit considering the growing issue of a lack of available grid capacity. Earlier this year, Toby Virno, investment manager at Foresight Solar Fund, wrote a blog for Solar Power Portal in which he highlighted that curtailment costs in the UK have ballooned to 2.3 billion as the UK commissions more renewable energy capacity than it has the grid infrastructure to effectively use.

value.Title: Tierra Adentro private equity firm introduces $100M tech infrastructure fund

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH1-CFP3-RYYH-G4W7-00000-00Tierra Adentro private equity firm introduces $100M tech infrastructure fund2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisTierra Adentro private equity firm introduces $100M tech infrastructure fundDylan Ortuno

Tierra Adentro, a New Mexico-based private equity firm, announced May 4 it would be launching and overseeing Tierra Adentro Fund, a $100 million infrastructure fund meant to help technology companies physically build in New Mexico.

"Entrepreneurs thinking about expanding in New Mexico are embracing the model we are building at Tierra Adentro," Managing Partner and Chief Investment Officer Alok Sindher said. "At the end of the day we are helping companies scale in a way traditional equity financing cannot and frankly should not. Founders can instead retain more autonomy and ownership of their company as they grow in, or expand to, New Mexico."

Sindher comes to Tierra Adentro Growth Capital after working for over 20 years in private equity. He was most recently a venture partner for head of infrastructure at Fifth Wall, a San Francisco-based asset manager focused on a mix of real estate and technology, and notably previously served as a Vice President in Investment Banking at J.P. Morgan.

The Tierra Adentro Fund is intended for investments ranging from a few million dollars to $40 million, according to the firm. So far, the company has already invested in geothermal energy developer Zanskar, based out of Wasatch Front in Utah, and expects to close another pair of investments soon.

Tierra Adentro Growth Capital will receive support from Alexander Harstrick and Jonathan Bronson, the founders of Boston-based venture capital firm J2 Ventures. The pair will serve as strategic advisors for the firm.

"The Tierra Adentro fund has more companies seeking investment than it can currently handle - with no marketing to date," Harstrick said. "This fund has the potential to create hundreds of high-wage jobs in the state and will duly ensure the State Investment Council's capital remains squarely focused on developing critical aspects of the state's future."

New Mexico SIC Director for Private Equity and Venture Capital Chris Cassidy said the new fund would benefit the state's technology ecosystem due to the "consolidation of major infrastructure platforms and the significant demand from the next generation of deep tech," the latter term referring to technology focused on global issues or with the ability to drastically change fundamental parts of living.

"The incentives and environment around building in New Mexico are already primed, but this fund's value proposition was missing," Cassidy said. "I am confident it will complement the SIC's broader investment strategy."

The SIC is Tierra Adentro Fund's primary investor. In the last three years, the SIC has committed a total of $1.8 billion to venture funds globally.

value.Title: Forest fire has a surprising goal: saving a rare species

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-24K3-RYCJ-G0XX-00000-00Forest fire has a surprising goal: saving a rare species2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:44:41ZLexisNexisAPg. A005Forest fire has a surprising goal: saving a rare speciesAustin MirminaStaff Writer

1) PHOTO: State environmental crews perform a controlled burn at Pachaug State Forest off Fish Road in Voluntown on Wednesday. (Dave Zajac/Hearst Connecticut Media)

VOLUNTOWN -- Waist-high flames crept across a 1-acre clearing Wednesday morning in Pachaug State Forest, steadily chewing through sticks, leaves and other woody debris in their path.

The fire was set by state forestry crews to clear leftover scraps from a recent timber harvest and create the kinds of bare conditions that pitch pine - a once-common tree species in Connecticut that officials say is now rare and at-risk - needs to regenerate.

By torching the forest floor, crews aimed to prepare the soil for pitch pine seeds to take hold and eventually grow, while also knocking back white pine and other trees that compete with pitch pine in Connecticut's largest state forest.

"We're hoping to prep the site in the best way we possibly can to get as much pitch pine as we can," said Michelle Auclair, a forester with the state Department of Energy and Environmental Protection, which conducted the controlled burn.

Pitch pine, recognizable for its chunky, alligator-like bark, used to be more common in Connecticut, thriving on sandy soils across the southeastern part of the state and along the coast, said Olney Knight, a DEEP fire control officer who oversee Wednesday's operation.

But housing and development have displaced much of that habitat because those same sandy areas are ideal for building, Auclair noted. Today, pitch pine forests are considered one of Connecticut's 13 imperiled ecosystems.

The land where Bradley International Airport now sits in Windsor Locks was once abundant with pitch pine, she said, adding that pockets of the species can still be found nearby.

Auclair said Cape Cod in Massachusetts "has a lot of pitch pine as well, but here in Connecticut, we only have a few strongholds left, and Pachaug is one of those places."

Pitch pine relies on disturbance to survive, Auclair said. Its cones are sealed with pitch and need high heat - often from fire - to open and release seeds. Without that, and without major weather events such as storms or wildfires to "take out the competition," the species struggles to regenerate.

"One of the reasons why we're so passionate about perpetuating this system in particular is because it's not able to self-perpetuate," Auclair said. "Left under normal circumstances, the white pine will outcompete the pitch pine and (Pachaug) will just transition to being a predominantly white pine forest."

The effort to restore pitch pine actually began last year, when a contractor hired by DEEP cleared about 20 acres of white pine and other competing species. The cut trees were chipped, sent to a plant and turned into a renewable energy source, officials said.

On Wednesday, 15 members of DEEP's forestry division returned to burn the remaining "slash" - branches, limbs and other woody debris left behind from the logging work.

Wearing helmets and sunglasses - but no masks - a crew of five or six workers used drip torches to ignite the edge of the 1-acre plot. Working in pairs, they walked the perimeter, marked by a bulldozed dirt path, and set two parallel lines of fire around the site.

Then they lit another line farther inside the clearing to guide the flames inward.

"You can steer fire with fire," said Steve Gramola, who was part of the ignitions team and also works as a DEEP carpenter.

As the burn intensified, the flames climbed several feet high, spreading evenly across the site. Thick plumes of smoke rose above the still-standing trees.

At one point, a narrow column of flame - a "fire whirl," according to the burn leader Rich Schenk - twisted above the blaze. He said it was caused by uneven heating at the surface.

Nearby, other crew members stood ready with hoses in case they needed to put out spot fires that jumped the bulldozed line. One worker was stationed in a tracked utility vehicle equipped with a water tank.

Knight described the operation as a carefully coordinated "dance."

After about an hour, the flames died down, leaving behind a heavily charred, smoldering forest floor. Patches of green remained where the fire burned less intensely, forming what Knight described as a "burn mosaic."

The burn was something of a test for the forestry team, which is more accustomed to igniting fast-moving fuels like grasses than the heavier material found in Pachaug.

All in all, the fire burned better than crews expected.

The operation was one of several prescribed burns DEEP has done across the state this spring - from inland sand barrens in Windsor to oak savannas in Lyme and dry grassland in Mansfield.

DEEP officials say fire is an important habitat management tool used to eliminate invasive species, open up areas for rare habitats and remove excess fuel that can contribute to larger wildfires.

Wednesday's burn initially was supposed to cover all 20 acres of cleared forest, but officials scaled it back, saying a burn of the whole area would take too much time and wasn't necessary.

Tiny pitch pine seedlings could begin sprouting as soon as this summer.

"This is going to be a pretty neat ecosystem," Knight said.

value.Title: 'This is going to be a pretty neat ecosystem'

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-24K3-RYCJ-G0XV-00000-00'This is going to be a pretty neat ecosystem'2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:44:41ZLexisNexisAPg. A001'This is going to be a pretty neat ecosystem'Austin MirminaStaff Writer

1) PHOTO: Forester Michelle Auclair walks by a controlled burn performed by state environmental crews at Pachaug State Forest off Fish Road in Voluntown on Wednesday. (Dave Zajac/Hearst Connecticut Media)

VOLUNTOWN -- Waist-high flames crept across a 1-acre clearing Wednesday morning in Pachaug State Forest, steadily chewing through sticks, leaves and other woody debris in their path.

The fire was set by state forestry crews to clear leftover scraps from a recent timber harvest and create the kinds of bare conditions that pitch pine - a once-common tree species in Connecticut that officials say is now rare and at-risk - needs to regenerate.

By torching the forest floor, crews aimed to prepare the soil for pitch pine seeds to take hold and eventually grow, while also knocking back white pine and other trees that compete with pitch pine in Connecticut's largest state forest.

"We're hoping to prep the site in the best way we possibly can to get as much pitch pine as we can," said Michelle Auclair, a forester with the state Department of Energy and Environmental Protection, which conducted the controlled burn.

Pitch pine, recognizable for its chunky, alligator-like bark, used to be more common in Connecticut, thriving on sandy soils across the southeastern part of the state and along the coast, said Olney Knight, a DEEP fire control officer who oversee Wednesday's operation.

But housing and development have displaced much of that habitat because those same sandy areas are ideal for building, Auclair noted. Today, pitch pine forests are considered one of Connecticut's 13 imperiled ecosystems.

The land where Bradley International Airport now sits in Windsor Locks was once abundant with pitch pine, she said, adding that pockets of the species can still be found nearby.

Auclair said Cape Cod in Massachusetts "has a lot of pitch pine as well, but here in Connecticut, we only have a few strongholds left, and Pachaug is one of those places."

Pitch pine relies on disturbance to survive, Auclair said. Its cones are sealed with pitch and need high heat - often from fire - to open and release seeds. Without that, and without major weather events such as storms or wildfires to "take out the competition," the species struggles to regenerate.

"One of the reasons why we're so passionate about perpetuating this system in particular is because it's not able to self-perpetuate," Auclair said. "Left under normal circumstances, the white pine will outcompete the pitch pine and (Pachaug) will just transition to being a predominantly white pine forest."

The effort to restore pitch pine actually began last year, when a contractor hired by DEEP cleared about 20 acres of white pine and other competing species. The cut trees were chipped, sent to a plant and turned into a renewable energy source, officials said.

On Wednesday, 15 members of DEEP's forestry division returned to burn the remaining "slash" - branches, limbs and other woody debris left behind from the logging work.

Wearing helmets and sunglasses - but no masks - a crew of five or six workers used drip torches to ignite the edge of the 1-acre plot. Working in pairs, they walked the perimeter, marked by a bulldozed dirt path, and set two parallel lines of fire around the site.

Then they lit another line farther inside the clearing to guide the flames inward.

"You can steer fire with fire," said Steve Gramola, who was part of the ignitions team and also works as a DEEP carpenter.

As the burn intensified, the flames climbed several feet high, spreading evenly across the site. Thick plumes of smoke rose above the still-standing trees.

At one point, a narrow column of flame - a "fire whirl," according to the burn leader Rich Schenk - twisted above the blaze. He said it was caused by uneven heating at the surface.

Nearby, other crew members stood ready with hoses in case they needed to put out spot fires that jumped the bulldozed line. One worker was stationed in a tracked utility vehicle equipped with a water tank.

Knight described the operation as a carefully coordinated "dance."

After about an hour, the flames died down, leaving behind a heavily charred, smoldering forest floor. Patches of green remained where the fire burned less intensely, forming what Knight described as a "burn mosaic."

The burn was something of a test for the forestry team, which is more accustomed to igniting fast-moving fuels like grasses than the heavier material found in Pachaug.

All in all, the fire burned better than crews expected.

The operation was one of several prescribed burns DEEP has done across the state this spring - from inland sand barrens in Windsor to oak savannas in Lyme and dry grassland in Mansfield.

DEEP officials say fire is an important habitat management tool used to eliminate invasive species, open up areas for rare habitats and remove excess fuel that can contribute to larger wildfires.

Wednesday's burn initially was supposed to cover all 20 acres of cleared forest, but officials scaled it back, saying a burn of the whole area would take too much time and wasn't necessary.

Tiny pitch pine seedlings could begin sprouting as soon as this summer.

"This is going to be a pretty neat ecosystem," Knight said.

value.Title: Controlled fire aims to save rare species

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-24K3-RYCJ-G0XW-00000-00Controlled fire aims to save rare species2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:44:42ZLexisNexisAPg. A001Controlled fire aims to save rare speciesAustin MirminaStaff Writer

1) PHOTO: Forester Michelle Auclair walks by a controlled burn performed by state environmental crews at Pachaug State Forest off Fish Road in Voluntown on Wednesday. (Dave Zajac/Hearst Connecticut Media)

VOLUNTOWN -- Waist-high flames crept across a 1-acre clearing Wednesday morning in Pachaug State Forest, steadily chewing through sticks, leaves and other woody debris in their path.

The fire was set by state forestry crews to clear leftover scraps from a recent timber harvest and create the kinds of bare conditions that pitch pine - a once-common tree species in Connecticut that officials say is now rare and at-risk - needs to regenerate.

By torching the forest floor, crews aimed to prepare the soil for pitch pine seeds to take hold and eventually grow, while also knocking back white pine and other trees that compete with pitch pine in Connecticut's largest state forest.

"We're hoping to prep the site in the best way we possibly can to get as much pitch pine as we can," said Michelle Auclair, a forester with the state Department of Energy and Environmental Protection, which conducted the controlled burn.

Pitch pine, recognizable for its chunky, alligator-like bark, used to be more common in Connecticut, thriving on sandy soils across the southeastern part of the state and along the coast, said Olney Knight, a DEEP fire control officer who oversee Wednesday's operation.

But housing and development have displaced much of that habitat because those same sandy areas are ideal for building, Auclair noted. Today, pitch pine forests are considered one of Connecticut's 13 imperiled ecosystems.

The land where Bradley International Airport now sits in Windsor Locks was once abundant with pitch pine, she said, adding that pockets of the species can still be found nearby.

Auclair said Cape Cod in Massachusetts "has a lot of pitch pine as well, but here in Connecticut, we only have a few strongholds left, and Pachaug is one of those places."

Pitch pine relies on disturbance to survive, Auclair said. Its cones are sealed with pitch and need high heat - often from fire - to open and release seeds. Without that, and without major weather events such as storms or wildfires to "take out the competition," the species struggles to regenerate.

"One of the reasons why we're so passionate about perpetuating this system in particular is because it's not able to self-perpetuate," Auclair said. "Left under normal circumstances, the white pine will outcompete the pitch pine and (Pachaug) will just transition to being a predominantly white pine forest."

The effort to restore pitch pine actually began last year, when a contractor hired by DEEP cleared about 20 acres of white pine and other competing species. The cut trees were chipped, sent to a plant and turned into a renewable energy source, officials said.

On Wednesday, 15 members of DEEP's forestry division returned to burn the remaining "slash" - branches, limbs and other woody debris left behind from the logging work.

Wearing helmets and sunglasses - but no masks - a crew of five or six workers used drip torches to ignite the edge of the 1-acre plot. Working in pairs, they walked the perimeter, marked by a bulldozed dirt path, and set two parallel lines of fire around the site.

Then they lit another line farther inside the clearing to guide the flames inward.

"You can steer fire with fire," said Steve Gramola, who was part of the ignitions team and also works as a DEEP carpenter.

As the burn intensified, the flames climbed several feet high, spreading evenly across the site. Thick plumes of smoke rose above the still-standing trees.

At one point, a narrow column of flame - a "fire whirl," according to the burn leader Rich Schenk - twisted above the blaze. He said it was caused by uneven heating at the surface.

Nearby, other crew members stood ready with hoses in case they needed to put out spot fires that jumped the bulldozed line. One worker was stationed in a tracked utility vehicle equipped with a water tank.

Knight described the operation as a carefully coordinated "dance."

After about an hour, the flames died down, leaving behind a heavily charred, smoldering forest floor. Patches of green remained where the fire burned less intensely, forming what Knight described as a "burn mosaic."

The burn was something of a test for the forestry team, which is more accustomed to igniting fast-moving fuels like grasses than the heavier material found in Pachaug.

All in all, the fire burned better than crews expected.

The operation was one of several prescribed burns DEEP has done across the state this spring - from inland sand barrens in Windsor to oak savannas in Lyme and dry grassland in Mansfield.

DEEP officials say fire is an important habitat management tool used to eliminate invasive species, open up areas for rare habitats and remove excess fuel that can contribute to larger wildfires.

Wednesday's burn initially was supposed to cover all 20 acres of cleared forest, but officials scaled it back, saying a burn of the whole area would take too much time and wasn't necessary.

Tiny pitch pine seedlings could begin sprouting as soon as this summer.

"This is going to be a pretty neat ecosystem," Knight said.

value.Title: 'This is going to be a pretty neat ecosystem'

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-24K3-RYCJ-G0Y2-00000-00'This is going to be a pretty neat ecosystem'2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:44:41ZLexisNexisAPg. A001'This is going to be a pretty neat ecosystem'Austin MirminaStaff Writer

1) PHOTO: Forester Michelle Auclair walks by a controlled burn performed by state environmental crews at Pachaug State Forest off Fish Road in Voluntown on Wednesday. (Dave Zajac/Hearst Connecticut Media)

VOLUNTOWN -- Waist-high flames crept across a 1-acre clearing Wednesday morning in Pachaug State Forest, steadily chewing through sticks, leaves and other woody debris in their path.

The fire was set by state forestry crews to clear leftover scraps from a recent timber harvest and create the kinds of bare conditions that pitch pine - a once-common tree species in Connecticut that officials say is now rare and at-risk - needs to regenerate.

By torching the forest floor, crews aimed to prepare the soil for pitch pine seeds to take hold and eventually grow, while also knocking back white pine and other trees that compete with pitch pine in Connecticut's largest state forest.

"We're hoping to prep the site in the best way we possibly can to get as much pitch pine as we can," said Michelle Auclair, a forester with the state Department of Energy and Environmental Protection, which conducted the controlled burn.

Pitch pine, recognizable for its chunky, alligator-like bark, used to be more common in Connecticut, thriving on sandy soils across the southeastern part of the state and along the coast, said Olney Knight, a DEEP fire control officer who oversee Wednesday's operation.

But housing and development have displaced much of that habitat because those same sandy areas are ideal for building, Auclair noted. Today, pitch pine forests are considered one of Connecticut's 13 imperiled ecosystems.

The land where Bradley International Airport now sits in Windsor Locks was once abundant with pitch pine, she said, adding that pockets of the species can still be found nearby.

Auclair said Cape Cod in Massachusetts "has a lot of pitch pine as well, but here in Connecticut, we only have a few strongholds left, and Pachaug is one of those places."

Pitch pine relies on disturbance to survive, Auclair said. Its cones are sealed with pitch and need high heat - often from fire - to open and release seeds. Without that, and without major weather events such as storms or wildfires to "take out the competition," the species struggles to regenerate.

"One of the reasons why we're so passionate about perpetuating this system in particular is because it's not able to self-perpetuate," Auclair said. "Left under normal circumstances, the white pine will outcompete the pitch pine and (Pachaug) will just transition to being a predominantly white pine forest."

The effort to restore pitch pine actually began last year, when a contractor hired by DEEP cleared about 20 acres of white pine and other competing species. The cut trees were chipped, sent to a plant and turned into a renewable energy source, officials said.

On Wednesday, 15 members of DEEP's forestry division returned to burn the remaining "slash" - branches, limbs and other woody debris left behind from the logging work.

Wearing helmets and sunglasses - but no masks - a crew of five or six workers used drip torches to ignite the edge of the 1-acre plot. Working in pairs, they walked the perimeter, marked by a bulldozed dirt path, and set two parallel lines of fire around the site.

Then they lit another line farther inside the clearing to guide the flames inward.

"You can steer fire with fire," said Steve Gramola, who was part of the ignitions team and also works as a DEEP carpenter.

As the burn intensified, the flames climbed several feet high, spreading evenly across the site. Thick plumes of smoke rose above the still-standing trees.

At one point, a narrow column of flame - a "fire whirl," according to the burn leader Rich Schenk - twisted above the blaze. He said it was caused by uneven heating at the surface.

Nearby, other crew members stood ready with hoses in case they needed to put out spot fires that jumped the bulldozed line. One worker was stationed in a tracked utility vehicle equipped with a water tank.

Knight described the operation as a carefully coordinated "dance."

After about an hour, the flames died down, leaving behind a heavily charred, smoldering forest floor. Patches of green remained where the fire burned less intensely, forming what Knight described as a "burn mosaic."

The burn was something of a test for the forestry team, which is more accustomed to igniting fast-moving fuels like grasses than the heavier material found in Pachaug.

All in all, the fire burned better than crews expected.

The operation was one of several prescribed burns DEEP has done across the state this spring - from inland sand barrens in Windsor to oak savannas in Lyme and dry grassland in Mansfield.

DEEP officials say fire is an important habitat management tool used to eliminate invasive species, open up areas for rare habitats and remove excess fuel that can contribute to larger wildfires.

Wednesday's burn initially was supposed to cover all 20 acres of cleared forest, but officials scaled it back, saying a burn of the whole area would take too much time and wasn't necessary.

Tiny pitch pine seedlings could begin sprouting as soon as this summer.

"This is going to be a pretty neat ecosystem," Knight said.

value.Title: Federal Register Notice: Marine Renewable Energy Collaborative of New England, Inc.; Notice of Preliminary Permit Application Accepted for Filing and Soliciting Comments, Motions To Intervene, and Competing Applications (Updated on 05-May-2026)

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-BKC3-S0RF-C3NR-00000-00Federal Register Notice: Marine Renewable Energy Collaborative of New England, Inc.; Notice of Preliminary Permit Application Accepted for Filing and Soliciting Comments, Motions To Intervene, and Competing Applications (Updated on 05-May-2026)2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisFederal Register Notice: Marine Renewable Energy Collaborative of New England, Inc.; Notice of Preliminary Permit Application Accepted for Filing and Soliciting Comments, Motions To Intervene, and Competing Applications (Updated on 05-May-2026)

Washington: Office of the Federal Register has issued the following notice:

On April 3, 2026, Marine Renewable Energy Collaborative of New England, Inc., filed an application for a preliminary permit, pursuant to section 4(f) of the Federal Power Act, proposing to study the feasibility of the Cuttyhunk Test Range Project (Cuttyhunk Project or project), to be located in Vinyard Sound, approximately 2,000 feet off the south coast of Cuttyhunk Island, near the Town of Gosnold, Dukes County, Massachusetts. The sole purpose of a preliminary permit, if issued, is to grant the permit holder priority to file a license application during the permit term. A preliminary permit does not authorize the permit holder to perform any land-disturbing activities or otherwise enter upon lands or waters owned by others without the owners' express permission.

The proposed Cuttyhunk Project would consist of the following new features: (1) a wave energy converter structure with buoy; (2) a second buoy for a data acquisition system; (3) a three-anchored catenary mooring system; (4) a 5,280 foot buried submarine cable; (5) a communications hub on Cuttyhunk Island for monitoring the test range and receiving data; (6) a small solar photovoltaic system and battery; and (7) a cellular and satellite communications system for receiving data from the data acquisition system. The proposed project would have an estimated average annual generation of 200 megawatt-hours.

Applicant Contact: Stephen Barrett, Barrett Energy Resources Group, 10 River Run, Williamstown, MA 01267; phone: (339) 234-2696; email: steve@barrettenergygroup.com

FERC Contact: Robert Haltner; phone: (202) 502-8612, or by email at robert.haltner@ferc.gov

Deadline for filing comments, motions to intervene, competing applications (without notices of intent), or notices of ( printed page 24224) intent to file competing applications: on or before 5:00 p.m Eastern Time on June 29, 2026. Competing applications and notices of intent must meet the requirements of 18 CFR 4.36

The Commission strongly encourages electronic filing. Please file comments, motions to intervene, notices of intent, and competing applications using the Commission's eFiling system at https://ferconline.ferc.gov/ eFiling.aspx Commenters can submit brief comments up to 10,000 characters, without prior registration, using the eComment system at https://ferconline.ferc.gov/ QuickComment.aspx For assistance, please contact FERC Online Support at FERCOnlineSupport@ferc.gov, (866) 208-3676 (toll free), or (202) 502-8659 (TTY). In lieu of electronic filing, you may submit a paper copy. Submissions sent via the U.S Postal Service must be addressed to: Debbie-Anne A. Reese, Secretary, Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, 888 First Street NE, Room 1A, Washington, DC 20426. Submissions sent via any other carrier must be addressed to: Debbie-Anne A. Reese, Secretary, Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, 12225 Wilkins Avenue, Rockville, Maryland 20852. The first page of any filing should include docket number P-15426-000.

For public inquiries and assistance with intervening or participating, contact the Office of Public Participation at (202) 502-6595 or OPP@ferc.gov

More information about this project, including a copy of the application, can be viewed on the Commission's website ( http://www.ferc.gov) using the “eLibrary” link. Enter the docket number, excluding the last three digits (P-15426), in the docket number field to access the document. For assistance, contact FERC Online Support.

(Authority: 18 CFR 2.1)

Dated: April 29, 2026.

Debbie-Anne A. Reese,

Secretary.

[FR Doc. 2026-08655 Filed 5-4-26; 8:45 am]

BILLING CODE 6717-01-P

urn:contentItem:6JGY-HS03-SH7R-F1RM-00000-00Forest fire has a surprising goal: saving a rare species2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:44:41ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. A005Forest fire has a surprising goal: saving a rare speciesAustin MirminaSTAFF WRITER STAFF WRITER

VOLUNTOWN -- Waist-high flames crept across a 1-acre clearing Wednesday morning in Pachaug State Forest, steadily chewing through sticks, leaves and other woody debris in their path.

The fire was set by state forestry crews to clear leftover scraps from a recent timber harvest and create the kinds of bare conditions that pitch pine - a once-common tree species in Connecticut that officials say is now rare and at-risk - needs to regenerate.

By torching the forest floor, crews aimed to prepare the soil for pitch pine seeds to take hold and eventually grow, while also knocking back white pine and other trees that compete with pitch pine in Connecticut's largest state forest.

"We're hoping to prep the site in the best way we possibly can to get as much pitch pine as we can," said Michelle Auclair, a forester with the state Department of Energy and Environmental Protection, which conducted the controlled burn.

Pitch pine, recognizable for its chunky, alligator-like bark, used to be more common in Connecticut, thriving on sandy soils across the southeastern part of the state and along the coast, said Olney Knight, a DEEP fire control officer who oversee Wednesday's operation.

But housing and development have displaced much of that habitat because those same sandy areas are ideal for building, Auclair noted. Today, pitch pine forests are considered one of Connecticut's 13 imperiled ecosystems.

The land where Bradley International Airport now sits in Windsor Locks was once abundant with pitch pine, she said, adding that pockets of the species can still be found nearby.

Auclair said Cape Cod in Massachusetts "has a lot of pitch pine as well, but here in Connecticut, we only have a few strongholds left, and Pachaug is one of those places."

Pitch pine relies on disturbance to survive, Auclair said. Its cones are sealed with pitch and need high heat - often from fire - to open and release seeds. Without that, and without major weather events such as storms or wildfires to "take out the competition," the species struggles to regenerate.

"One of the reasons why we're so passionate about perpetuating this system in particular is because it's not able to self-perpetuate," Auclair said. "Left under normal circumstances, the white pine will outcompete the pitch pine and (Pachaug) will just transition to being a predominantly white pine forest."

The effort to restore pitch pine actually began last year, when a contractor hired by DEEP cleared about 20 acres of white pine and other competing species. The cut trees were chipped, sent to a plant and turned into a renewable energy source, officials said.

On Wednesday, 15 members of DEEP's forestry division returned to burn the remaining "slash" - branches, limbs and other woody debris left behind from the logging work.

Wearing helmets and sunglasses - but no masks - a crew of five or six workers used drip torches to ignite the edge of the 1-acre plot. Working in pairs, they walked the perimeter, marked by a bulldozed dirt path, and set two parallel lines of fire around the site.

Then they lit another line farther inside the clearing to guide the flames inward.

"You can steer fire with fire," said Steve Gramola, who was part of the ignitions team and also works as a DEEP carpenter.

As the burn intensified, the flames climbed several feet high, spreading evenly across the site. Thick plumes of smoke rose above the still-standing trees.

At one point, a narrow column of flame - a "fire whirl," according to the burn leader Rich Schenk - twisted above the blaze. He said it was caused by uneven heating at the surface.

Nearby, other crew members stood ready with hoses in case they needed to put out spot fires that jumped the bulldozed line. One worker was stationed in a tracked utility vehicle equipped with a water tank.

Knight described the operation as a carefully coordinated "dance."

After about an hour, the flames died down, leaving behind a heavily charred, smoldering forest floor. Patches of green remained where the fire burned less intensely, forming what Knight described as a "burn mosaic."

The burn was something of a test for the forestry team, which is more accustomed to igniting fast-moving fuels like grasses than the heavier material found in Pachaug.

All in all, the fire burned better than crews expected.

The operation was one of several prescribed burns DEEP has done across the state this spring - from inland sand barrens in Windsor to oak savannas in Lyme and dry grassland in Mansfield.

DEEP officials say fire is an important habitat management tool used to eliminate invasive species, open up areas for rare habitats and remove excess fuel that can contribute to larger wildfires.

Wednesday's burn initially was supposed to cover all 20 acres of cleared forest, but officials scaled it back, saying a burn of the whole area would take too much time and wasn't necessary.

Tiny pitch pine seedlings could begin sprouting as soon as this summer.

"This is going to be a pretty neat ecosystem," Knight said.

urn:contentItem:6JHF-4Y93-SC0J-6160-00000-00GE Vernova Stock Outlook: Is Wall Street Bullish or Bearish?2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisENGINEERING NEWS, Equity markets news & GENERAL ELECTRIC NEWSGE Vernova Stock Outlook: Is Wall Street Bullish or Bearish?Sohini Mondal

Valued at a market cap of $294.3 billion, GE Vernova Inc. (GEV) provides products and services to generate, transfer, convert, and store electricity across global markets. It operates through three segments: Power; Wind; and Electrification, offering technologies ranging from gas and nuclear systems to renewable energy and grid solutions.

Shares of the sustainable energy giant have significantly outperformed the broader market over the past 52 weeks. GEV stock has surged 167.4% over this time frame, while the broader S&P 500 Index ($SPX) has rallied 30.4%. On a YTD basis, shares of the company are up 64.2%, compared to SPX's 6.8% gain.

More Top Stocks Daily:

Go behind Wall Street's hottest headlines with Barchart's Active Investor newsletter.

 

Focusing more closely, shares of the Cambridge, Massachusetts-based company have outpaced the State Street Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF's (XLI) 30.7% return over the past 52 weeks. 

Shares of GE Vernova surged 13.8% on Apr. 22 after the company reported strong Q1 2026 results, including orders of $18.3 billion (up 71% organically), revenue of $9.3 billion (up 16%), and backlog growth of $13 billion to around $163 billion. Investor optimism was further driven by profitability and cash strength, with net income of $4.7 billion (50.9% margin), adjusted EBITDA of $900 million (nearly doubling year-over-year), and free cash flow of $4.8 billion, more than quadrupling from last year. 

The rally was reinforced by raised 2026 guidance, growing demand for power and electrification solutions, and strategic expansion including the Prolec GE acquisition and expectations for gas power backlog to reach at least 110 GW by year-end.

For the fiscal year ending in December 2026, analysts expect GEV's EPS to decline 16.1% year-over-year to $14.84. The company's earnings surprise history is mixed. It topped the consensus estimates in three of the last four quarters while missing on another occasion.

Among the 30 analysts covering the stock, the consensus rating is a "Strong Buy." That's based on 23 "Strong Buy" ratings, two "Moderate Buys," and five "Holds."

On Apr. 27, John Eade of Argus raised the price target on GE Vernova to $1,300 with a "Buy" rating.

The mean price target of $1,204.74 represents a 11.6% premium to GEV's current price levels. The Street-high price target of $1,400 suggests a 29.6% potential upside. 

On the date of publication, Sohini Mondal did not have (either directly or indirectly) positions in any of the securities mentioned in this article. All information and data in this article is solely for informational purposes. For more information please view the Barchart Disclosure Policy here.

value.Title: Controlled burn in state forest hopes to save rare species

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-24K3-RYCJ-G0Y3-00000-00Controlled burn in state forest hopes to save rare species2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:44:41ZLexisNexisAPg. A004Controlled burn in state forest hopes to save rare speciesAustin MirminaStaff Writer

1) PHOTO: Forester Michelle Auclair walks by a controlled burn performed by state environmental crews at Pachaug State Forest off Fish Road in Voluntown on Wednesday. 2) PHOTO: State environmental crews perform a controlled burn at Pachaug State Forest off Fish Road in Voluntown on Wednesday. 3) PHOTO: The burn helps protect pitch pine habitat by slowing the growth of white pine and other trees that compete with it. 4) PHOTO: State environmental crews perform a controlled burn at Pachaug State Forest off Fish Road in Voluntown on Wednesday. The burn helps protect pitch pine habitat by slowing the growth of white pine and other trees that compete with it. 5) PHOTO: A state worker uses a drip torch as crews perform a controlled burn at Pachaug State Forest off Fish Road in Voluntown on Wednesday. (Photos by Dave Zajac/Hearst Connecticut Media)

VOLUNTOWN -- Waist-high flames crept across a 1-acre clearing Wednesday morning in Pachaug State Forest, steadily chewing through sticks, leaves and other woody debris in their path.

The fire was set by state forestry crews to clear leftover scraps from a recent timber harvest and create the kinds of bare conditions that pitch pine - a once-common tree species in Connecticut that officials say is now rare and at-risk - needs to regenerate.

By torching the forest floor, crews aimed to prepare the soil for pitch pine seeds to take hold and eventually grow, while also knocking back white pine and other trees that compete with pitch pine in Connecticut's largest state forest.

"We're hoping to prep the site in the best way we possibly can to get as much pitch pine as we can," said Michelle Auclair, a forester with the state Department of Energy and Environmental Protection, which conducted the controlled burn.

Pitch pine, recognizable for its chunky, alligator-like bark, used to be more common in Connecticut, thriving on sandy soils across the southeastern part of the state and along the coast, said Olney Knight, a DEEP fire control officer who oversee Wednesday's operation.

But housing and development have displaced much of that habitat because those same sandy areas are ideal for building, Auclair noted. Today, pitch pine forests are considered one of Connecticut's 13 imperiled ecosystems.

The land where Bradley International Airport now sits in Windsor Locks was once abundant with pitch pine, she said, adding that pockets of the species can still be found nearby.

Auclair said Cape Cod in Massachusetts "has a lot of pitch pine as well, but here in Connecticut, we only have a few strongholds left, and Pachaug is one of those places."

Pitch pine relies on disturbance to survive, Auclair said. Its cones are sealed with pitch and need high heat - often from fire - to open and release seeds. Without that, and without major weather events such as storms or wildfires to "take out the competition," the species struggles to regenerate.

"One of the reasons why we're so passionate about perpetuating this system in particular is because it's not able to self-perpetuate," Auclair said. "Left under normal circumstances, the white pine will outcompete the pitch pine and (Pachaug) will just transition to being a predominantly white pine forest."

The effort to restore pitch pine actually began last year, when a contractor hired by DEEP cleared about 20 acres of white pine and other competing species. The cut trees were chipped, sent to a plant and turned into a renewable energy source, officials said.

On Wednesday, 15 members of DEEP's forestry division returned to burn the remaining "slash" - branches, limbs and other woody debris left behind from the logging work.

Wearing helmets and sunglasses - but no masks - a crew of five or six workers used drip torches to ignite the edge of the 1-acre plot. Working in pairs, they walked the perimeter, marked by a bulldozed dirt path, and set two parallel lines of fire around the site.

Then they lit another line farther inside the clearing to guide the flames inward.

"You can steer fire with fire," said Steve Gramola, who was part of the ignitions team and also works as a DEEP carpenter.

As the burn intensified, the flames climbed several feet high, spreading evenly across the site. Thick plumes of smoke rose above the still-standing trees.

At one point, a narrow column of flame - a "fire whirl," according to the burn leader Rich Schenk - twisted above the blaze. He said it was caused by uneven heating at the surface.

Nearby, other crew members stood ready with hoses in case they needed to put out spot fires that jumped the bulldozed line. One worker was stationed in a tracked utility vehicle equipped with a water tank.

Knight described the operation as a carefully coordinated "dance."

After about an hour, the flames died down, leaving behind a heavily charred, smoldering forest floor. Patches of green remained where the fire burned less intensely, forming what Knight described as a "burn mosaic."

The burn was something of a test for the forestry team, which is more accustomed to igniting fast-moving fuels like grasses than the heavier material found in Pachaug.

All in all, the fire burned better than crews expected.

The operation was one of several prescribed burns DEEP has done across the state this spring - from inland sand barrens in Windsor to oak savannas in Lyme and dry grassland in Mansfield.

DEEP officials say fire is an important habitat management tool used to eliminate invasive species, open up areas for rare habitats and remove excess fuel that can contribute to larger wildfires.

Wednesday's burn initially was supposed to cover all 20 acres of cleared forest, but officials scaled it back, saying a burn of the whole area would take too much time and wasn't necessary.

Tiny pitch pine seedlings could begin sprouting as soon as this summer.

"This is going to be a pretty neat ecosystem," Knight said.

urn:contentItem:6JGY-24K3-RYCJ-G0XY-00000-00Conn. forest fire has a surprising goal2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:44:41ZLexisNexisAPg. A003Conn. forest fire has a surprising goalAustin MirminaStaff Writer

1) PHOTO: Forester Michelle Auclair walks by a controlled burn performed by state environmental crews at Pachaug State Forest off Fish Road in Voluntown on Wednesday. (Dave Zajac/Hearst Connecticut Media) 2) PHOTO: The burn helps protect pitch pine habitat by slowing the growth of white pine and other trees that compete with it. 3) PHOTO: State environmental crews perform a controlled burn at Pachaug State Forest off Fish Road in Voluntown on Wednesday. 4) PHOTO: A state worker uses a drip torch as crews perform a controlled burn at Pachaug State Forest off Fish Road in Voluntown on Wednesday.

VOLUNTOWN -- Waist-high flames crept across a 1-acre clearing Wednesday morning in Pachaug State Forest, steadily chewing through sticks, leaves and other woody debris in their path.

The fire was set by state forestry crews to clear leftover scraps from a recent timber harvest and create the kinds of bare conditions that pitch pine - a once-common tree species in Connecticut that officials say is now rare and at-risk - needs to regenerate.

By torching the forest floor, crews aimed to prepare the soil for pitch pine seeds to take hold and eventually grow, while also knocking back white pine and other trees that compete with pitch pine in Connecticut's largest state forest.

"We're hoping to prep the site in the best way we possibly can to get as much pitch pine as we can," said Michelle Auclair, a forester with the state Department of Energy and Environmental Protection, which conducted the controlled burn.

Pitch pine, recognizable for its chunky, alligator-like bark, used to be more common in Connecticut, thriving on sandy soils across the southeastern part of the state and along the coast, said Olney Knight, a DEEP fire control officer who oversee Wednesday's operation.

But housing and development have displaced much of that habitat because those same sandy areas are ideal for building, Auclair noted. Today, pitch pine forests are considered one of Connecticut's 13 imperiled ecosystems.

The land where Bradley International Airport now sits in Windsor Locks was once abundant with pitch pine, she said, adding that pockets of the species can still be found nearby.

Auclair said Cape Cod in Massachusetts "has a lot of pitch pine as well, but here in Connecticut, we only have a few strongholds left, and Pachaug is one of those places."

Pitch pine relies on disturbance to survive, Auclair said. Its cones are sealed with pitch and need high heat - often from fire - to open and release seeds. Without that, and without major weather events such as storms or wildfires to "take out the competition," the species struggles to regenerate.

"One of the reasons why we're so passionate about perpetuating this system in particular is because it's not able to self-perpetuate," Auclair said. "Left under normal circumstances, the white pine will outcompete the pitch pine and (Pachaug) will just transition to being a predominantly white pine forest."

The effort to restore pitch pine actually began last year, when a contractor hired by DEEP cleared about 20 acres of white pine and other competing species. The cut trees were chipped, sent to a plant and turned into a renewable energy source, officials said.

On Wednesday, 15 members of DEEP's forestry division returned to burn the remaining "slash" - branches, limbs and other woody debris left behind from the logging work.

Wearing helmets and sunglasses - but no masks - a crew of five or six workers used drip torches to ignite the edge of the 1-acre plot. Working in pairs, they walked the perimeter, marked by a bulldozed dirt path, and set two parallel lines of fire around the site.

Then they lit another line farther inside the clearing to guide the flames inward.

"You can steer fire with fire," said Steve Gramola, who was part of the ignitions team and also works as a DEEP carpenter.

As the burn intensified, the flames climbed several feet high, spreading evenly across the site. Thick plumes of smoke rose above the still-standing trees.

At one point, a narrow column of flame - a "fire whirl," according to the burn leader Rich Schenk - twisted above the blaze. He said it was caused by uneven heating at the surface.

Nearby, other crew members stood ready with hoses in case they needed to put out spot fires that jumped the bulldozed line. One worker was stationed in a tracked utility vehicle equipped with a water tank.

Knight described the operation as a carefully coordinated "dance."

After about an hour, the flames died down, leaving behind a heavily charred, smoldering forest floor. Patches of green remained where the fire burned less intensely, forming what Knight described as a "burn mosaic."

The burn was something of a test for the forestry team, which is more accustomed to igniting fast-moving fuels like grasses than the heavier material found in Pachaug.

All in all, the fire burned better than crews expected.

The operation was one of several prescribed burns DEEP has done across the state this spring - from inland sand barrens in Windsor to oak savannas in Lyme and dry grassland in Mansfield.

DEEP officials say fire is an important habitat management tool used to eliminate invasive species, open up areas for rare habitats and remove excess fuel that can contribute to larger wildfires.

Wednesday's burn initially was supposed to cover all 20 acres of cleared forest, but officials scaled it back, saying a burn of the whole area would take too much time and wasn't necessary.

Tiny pitch pine seedlings could begin sprouting as soon as this summer.

"This is going to be a pretty neat ecosystem," Knight said.

value.Title: FirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK7-WS93-RV1C-S2K8-00000-00FirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisFirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites

ISN, a provider of contractor and supplier information management services, reported FirstLight, an independent power producer, has extended its use of ISNetworld to operations in Quebec and Ontario.

ISN said it will continue to help FirstLight streamline contractor management and consistently communicate safety expectations across North America.

"By partnering with ISN, we can more consistently manage compliance across various local and federal regulations while maintaining safety standards throughout North America," said PattyGoclowski, Director, Health, Safety & Environmental (HSE) at FirstLight. "Implementing ISNetworld at additional FirstLight sites in Canada helps enable staff to focus on activities on-site while ensuring contractors meet both regulatory and insurance requirements before arrival, upholding rigorous safety expectations."

According to a release, headquartered in Burlington, Massachusetts with offices in Montreal, Quebec, and Oshawa, Ontario, FirstLight operates renewable energy assets encompassing hydro, solar, and battery energy storage. ISN helps support FirstLight's goal of maintaining consistent safety standards through the use of ISNetworld tools and services. With the expansion to additional sites, the company will leverage ISNetworld's Online Training tool to help ensure contractors complete orientation requirements prior to arriving onsite and RAVS 360 to gain insights into contractors' HSE program implementation and safety culture, helping improve visibility into contractor performance, streamline onboarding, and align with evolving regulatory frameworks at local and federal levels.

"ISN is proud to support FirstLight as it continues to expand its renewable energy footprint in Canada," said Kim Ritchie, Senior Vice President of Canadian Operations at ISN. "By using ISNetworld to help increase consistency of contractor compliance practices, FirstLight exemplifies a proactive commitment to safety, operational efficiency, and environmental stewardship across its portfolio."

More information and complete details:

isn.com

firstlight.energy

value.Title: WEEKLY CORPORATE ACTIVITY REVIEW: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-2HJ3-SDM5-R2SP-00000-00WEEKLY CORPORATE ACTIVITY REVIEW: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisWEEKLY CORPORATE ACTIVITY REVIEW: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

+ RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC [3 Press Releases]

+ GMR SOLUTIONS REVEALS IPO TERMS FOR $750 MILLION OFFERING

GMR Solutions, a provider of emergency medical services and alternate-site care in the United States, has disclosed the terms of its upcoming initial public offering. The Lewisville, Texas-based company plans to raise $750 million through the sale of 31.9 million shares priced between $22 and $25 each. The information is sourced from the company's website.

+ PHASE 2 AUTOIMMUNE BIOTECH ODYSSEY THERAPEUTICS SETS IPO TERMS, AIMING TO RAISE ABOUT $232 MILLION

Odyssey Therapeutics, a Phase 2 biotech pursuing precision therapies for autoimmune diseases, has set terms for its initial public offering. The Boston, MA-based company intends to raise about $232 million by offering 13.2 million shares at $16 to $18 each. Odyssey's focus is on autoimmune and inflammatory conditions, applying immunobiology and drug discovery to identify signaling pathways that drive disease. Lead programs include oral small-molecule candidates targeting RIPK2 and SLC15A4, with its most advanced drug showing early efficacy in ulcerative colitis and others in preclinical development. The company aims to address inflammatory bowel disease and lupus, where treatment options remain limited. Source: Company Website

+ FERVO ENERGY UNVEILS TERMS FOR $1.2 BILLION GEOTHERMAL IPO

Fervo Energy, which is developing next-generation geothermal energy projects, announced terms for its IPO on Monday. The Houston, TX-based company plans to raise $1.2 billion by selling 55.6 million shares at a price range of $21 to $24. Cornerstone investors are slated to purchase $350 million worth of shares in the offering, representing 28% of the deal. Source: Company Website

value.Title: WEEKLY BUSINESS TRACKER: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-DVG3-S2B7-82WP-00000-00WEEKLY BUSINESS TRACKER: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisWEEKLY BUSINESS TRACKER: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

+ RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC [3 Press Releases]

+ GMR SOLUTIONS ANNOUNCES TERMS FOR $750 MILLION IPO, TARGETING EMS EXPANSION

GMR Solutions, a provider of emergency medical services and alternate-site care in the US, announced the terms of its IPO. The Lewisville, TX-based company plans to raise $750 million by offering 31.9 million shares at a price range of $22 to $25 per share. Source: Company Website

+ ODYSSEY THERAPEUTICS ANNOUNCES TERMS FOR ABOUT $232 MILLION IPO, PRICING RANGE SET AT $16-$18

Odyssey Therapeutics, a Phase 2 biotech developing precision therapies for autoimmune diseases, announced terms for its IPO. The Boston-based company plans to raise about $232 million by offering 13.2 million shares at a price range of $16 to $18 per share. Odyssey focuses on targeted therapies for autoimmune and inflammatory diseases, leveraging immunobiology and drug discovery to identify and inhibit key signaling pathways that drive disease. Its lead programs include oral small-molecule candidates targeting RIPK2 and SLC15A4, with its most advanced drug showing early efficacy in ulcerative colitis and others in preclinical development. Odyssey aims to address conditions such as inflammatory bowel disease and lupus, where treatment options remain limited. Source: Company Website

+ RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC SETS TERMS FOR FERVO ENERGY'S $1.2 BILLION GEOTHERMAL IPO

Fervo Energy, a Houston-based developer of next-generation geothermal energy projects, announced terms for its IPO on Monday. The company plans to raise $1.2 billion by offering 55.6 million shares at a price range of $21 to $24 per share. Cornerstone investors intend to purchase about $350 million worth of shares, representing 28% of the deal. Source: Company Website

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

value.Title: FirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK1-XWX3-RVY1-02TS-00000-00FirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisFirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites

ISN, a provider of contractor and supplier information management services, reported FirstLight, an independent power producer, has extended its use of ISNetworld to operations in Quebec and Ontario.

ISN said it will continue to help FirstLight streamline contractor management and consistently communicate safety expectations across North America.

"By partnering with ISN, we can more consistently manage compliance across various local and federal regulations while maintaining safety standards throughout North America," said PattyGoclowski, Director, Health, Safety & Environmental (HSE) at FirstLight. "Implementing ISNetworld at additional FirstLight sites in Canada helps enable staff to focus on activities on-site while ensuring contractors meet both regulatory and insurance requirements before arrival, upholding rigorous safety expectations."

According to a release, headquartered in Burlington, Massachusetts with offices in Montreal, Quebec, and Oshawa, Ontario, FirstLight operates renewable energy assets encompassing hydro, solar, and battery energy storage. ISN helps support FirstLight's goal of maintaining consistent safety standards through the use of ISNetworld tools and services. With the expansion to additional sites, the company will leverage ISNetworld's Online Training tool to help ensure contractors complete orientation requirements prior to arriving onsite and RAVS 360 to gain insights into contractors' HSE program implementation and safety culture, helping improve visibility into contractor performance, streamline onboarding, and align with evolving regulatory frameworks at local and federal levels.

"ISN is proud to support FirstLight as it continues to expand its renewable energy footprint in Canada," said Kim Ritchie, Senior Vice President of Canadian Operations at ISN. "By using ISNetworld to help increase consistency of contractor compliance practices, FirstLight exemplifies a proactive commitment to safety, operational efficiency, and environmental stewardship across its portfolio."

More information and complete details:

isn.com

firstlight.energy

((Comments on this story may be sent to newsdesk@closeupmedia.com))

value.Title: WEEKLY BUSINESS BRIEFING: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJN-FNH3-S0MP-430G-00000-00WEEKLY BUSINESS BRIEFING: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisWEEKLY BUSINESS BRIEFING: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

+ RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC [3 Press Releases]

+ GMR SOLUTIONS ANNOUNCES TERMS FOR $750 MILLION IPO, TARGETING EMS EXPANSION

GMR Solutions, a provider of emergency medical services and alternate-site care in the US, announced the terms of its IPO. The Lewisville, TX-based company plans to raise $750 million by offering 31.9 million shares at a price range of $22 to $25 per share. Source: Company Website

+ ODYSSEY THERAPEUTICS ANNOUNCES TERMS FOR ABOUT $232 MILLION IPO, PRICING RANGE SET AT $16-$18

Odyssey Therapeutics, a Phase 2 biotech developing precision therapies for autoimmune diseases, announced terms for its IPO. The Boston-based company plans to raise about $232 million by offering 13.2 million shares at a price range of $16 to $18 per share. Odyssey focuses on targeted therapies for autoimmune and inflammatory diseases, leveraging immunobiology and drug discovery to identify and inhibit key signaling pathways that drive disease. Its lead programs include oral small-molecule candidates targeting RIPK2 and SLC15A4, with its most advanced drug showing early efficacy in ulcerative colitis and others in preclinical development. Odyssey aims to address conditions such as inflammatory bowel disease and lupus, where treatment options remain limited. Source: Company Website

+ RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC SETS TERMS FOR FERVO ENERGY'S $1.2 BILLION GEOTHERMAL IPO

Fervo Energy, a Houston-based developer of next-generation geothermal energy projects, announced terms for its IPO on Monday. The company plans to raise $1.2 billion by offering 55.6 million shares at a price range of $21 to $24 per share. Cornerstone investors intend to purchase about $350 million worth of shares, representing 28% of the deal. Source: Company Website

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

value.Title: FirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJV-24X3-RTT6-44TV-00000-00FirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisFirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites

ISN, a provider of contractor and supplier information management services, reported FirstLight, an independent power producer, has extended its use of ISNetworld to operations in Quebec and Ontario.

ISN said it will continue to help FirstLight streamline contractor management and consistently communicate safety expectations across North America.

"By partnering with ISN, we can more consistently manage compliance across various local and federal regulations while maintaining safety standards throughout North America," said PattyGoclowski, Director, Health, Safety & Environmental (HSE) at FirstLight. "Implementing ISNetworld at additional FirstLight sites in Canada helps enable staff to focus on activities on-site while ensuring contractors meet both regulatory and insurance requirements before arrival, upholding rigorous safety expectations."

According to a release, headquartered in Burlington, Massachusetts with offices in Montreal, Quebec, and Oshawa, Ontario, FirstLight operates renewable energy assets encompassing hydro, solar, and battery energy storage. ISN helps support FirstLight's goal of maintaining consistent safety standards through the use of ISNetworld tools and services. With the expansion to additional sites, the company will leverage ISNetworld's Online Training tool to help ensure contractors complete orientation requirements prior to arriving onsite and RAVS 360 to gain insights into contractors' HSE program implementation and safety culture, helping improve visibility into contractor performance, streamline onboarding, and align with evolving regulatory frameworks at local and federal levels.

"ISN is proud to support FirstLight as it continues to expand its renewable energy footprint in Canada," said Kim Ritchie, Senior Vice President of Canadian Operations at ISN. "By using ISNetworld to help increase consistency of contractor compliance practices, FirstLight exemplifies a proactive commitment to safety, operational efficiency, and environmental stewardship across its portfolio."

More information and complete details:

isn.com

firstlight.energy

((Comments on this story may be sent to newsdesk@closeupmedia.com))

Lead programs include oral small-molecule candidates targeting RIPK2 and SLC15A4, with its most advanced drug showing early efficacy in ulcerative colitis and others in preclinical development. value.Title: FirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJM-0SX3-RRXD-2055-00000-00FirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisFirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites

ISN, a provider of contractor and supplier information management services, reported FirstLight, an independent power producer, has extended its use of ISNetworld to operations in Quebec and Ontario.

ISN said it will continue to help FirstLight streamline contractor management and consistently communicate safety expectations across North America.

"By partnering with ISN, we can more consistently manage compliance across various local and federal regulations while maintaining safety standards throughout North America," said PattyGoclowski, Director, Health, Safety & Environmental (HSE) at FirstLight. "Implementing ISNetworld at additional FirstLight sites in Canada helps enable staff to focus on activities on-site while ensuring contractors meet both regulatory and insurance requirements before arrival, upholding rigorous safety expectations."

According to a release, headquartered in Burlington, Massachusetts with offices in Montreal, Quebec, and Oshawa, Ontario, FirstLight operates renewable energy assets encompassing hydro, solar, and battery energy storage. ISN helps support FirstLight's goal of maintaining consistent safety standards through the use of ISNetworld tools and services. With the expansion to additional sites, the company will leverage ISNetworld's Online Training tool to help ensure contractors complete orientation requirements prior to arriving onsite and RAVS 360 to gain insights into contractors' HSE program implementation and safety culture, helping improve visibility into contractor performance, streamline onboarding, and align with evolving regulatory frameworks at local and federal levels.

"ISN is proud to support FirstLight as it continues to expand its renewable energy footprint in Canada," said Kim Ritchie, Senior Vice President of Canadian Operations at ISN. "By using ISNetworld to help increase consistency of contractor compliance practices, FirstLight exemplifies a proactive commitment to safety, operational efficiency, and environmental stewardship across its portfolio."

More information and complete details:

isn.com

firstlight.energy

((Comments on this story may be sent to newsdesk@closeupmedia.com))

value.Title: WEEKLY MARKET TRENDS UPDATE: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ2-6M83-RX03-V0FJ-00000-00WEEKLY MARKET TRENDS UPDATE: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisWEEKLY MARKET TRENDS UPDATE: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

+ RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC [3 Press Releases]

+ GMR SOLUTIONS REVEALS IPO TERMS FOR $750 MILLION OFFERING

GMR Solutions, a provider of emergency medical services and alternate-site care in the United States, has disclosed the terms of its upcoming initial public offering. The Lewisville, Texas-based company plans to raise $750 million through the sale of 31.9 million shares priced between $22 and $25 each. The information is sourced from the company's website.

+ PHASE 2 AUTOIMMUNE BIOTECH ODYSSEY THERAPEUTICS SETS IPO TERMS, AIMING TO RAISE ABOUT $232 MILLION

Odyssey Therapeutics, a Phase 2 biotech pursuing precision therapies for autoimmune diseases, has set terms for its initial public offering. The Boston, MA-based company intends to raise about $232 million by offering 13.2 million shares at $16 to $18 each. Odyssey's focus is on autoimmune and inflammatory conditions, applying immunobiology and drug discovery to identify signaling pathways that drive disease. Lead programs include oral small-molecule candidates targeting RIPK2 and SLC15A4, with its most advanced drug showing early efficacy in ulcerative colitis and others in preclinical development. The company aims to address inflammatory bowel disease and lupus, where treatment options remain limited. Source: Company Website

+ FERVO ENERGY UNVEILS TERMS FOR $1.2 BILLION GEOTHERMAL IPO

Fervo Energy, which is developing next-generation geothermal energy projects, announced terms for its IPO on Monday. The Houston, TX-based company plans to raise $1.2 billion by selling 55.6 million shares at a price range of $21 to $24. Cornerstone investors are slated to purchase $350 million worth of shares in the offering, representing 28% of the deal. Source: Company Website

value.Title: Mubadala invests in US renewable software firm Power Factors

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHT-X6S3-S9B9-13MF-00000-00Mubadala invests in US renewable software firm Power Factors2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisMubadala invests in US renewable software firm Power FactorsSeeNews

May 8 (Renewables Now) - Abu Dhabi's state-owned holding company Mubadala Investment Company has bought a minority stake in Power Factors LLC, a US provider of renewable energy operations and asset performance management software.

The investment will support the US firm's growth in the global market and help it expand its monitoring and control business, it said on Wednesday. Financial details about the transaction were kept under wraps.

"This investment helps us fulfill the next step in our mission to create the world's leading AI-driven suite of renewable energy management solutions, giving our customers everything they need to succeed as their portfolios grow," said Julieann Esper Rainville, CEO of Power Factors.

Massachusetts-based Power Factors is a renewable energy management software company (REMS) which provides a unified platform to monitor, analyse and manage wind, solar and energy storage assets. Its Unity Renewable Energy Management Suite deploys AI-driven intelligence to enhance the performance of power plants in real-time conditions. The company says it currently supports and optimises with its solutions roughly 310 GW of solar, wind and energy storage assets.

US private equity firm Vista Equity Partners is among Power Factors' backers, with a minority shareholding in the tech firm.

+ RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC [3 Press Releases]

+ GMR SOLUTIONS ANNOUNCES TERMS FOR $750 MILLION IPO, TARGETING EMS EXPANSION

GMR Solutions, a provider of emergency medical services and alternate-site care in the US, announced the terms of its IPO. The Lewisville, TX-based company plans to raise $750 million by offering 31.9 million shares at a price range of $22 to $25 per share. Source: Company Website

+ ODYSSEY THERAPEUTICS ANNOUNCES TERMS FOR ABOUT $232 MILLION IPO, PRICING RANGE SET AT $16-$18

Odyssey Therapeutics, a Phase 2 biotech developing precision therapies for autoimmune diseases, announced terms for its IPO. The Boston-based company plans to raise about $232 million by offering 13.2 million shares at a price range of $16 to $18 per share. Odyssey focuses on targeted therapies for autoimmune and inflammatory diseases, leveraging immunobiology and drug discovery to identify and inhibit key signaling pathways that drive disease. Its lead programs include oral small-molecule candidates targeting RIPK2 and SLC15A4, with its most advanced drug showing early efficacy in ulcerative colitis and others in preclinical development. Odyssey aims to address conditions such as inflammatory bowel disease and lupus, where treatment options remain limited. Source: Company Website

+ RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC SETS TERMS FOR FERVO ENERGY'S $1.2 BILLION GEOTHERMAL IPO

Fervo Energy, a Houston-based developer of next-generation geothermal energy projects, announced terms for its IPO on Monday. The company plans to raise $1.2 billion by offering 55.6 million shares at a price range of $21 to $24 per share. Cornerstone investors intend to purchase about $350 million worth of shares, representing 28% of the deal. Source: Company Website

value.Title: FirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ0-30Y3-SGJT-T45W-00000-00FirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisFirstLight Bolsters Partnership with ISN Across Additional Sites

ISN, a provider of contractor and supplier information management services, reported FirstLight, an independent power producer, has extended its use of ISNetworld to operations in Quebec and Ontario.

ISN said it will continue to help FirstLight streamline contractor management and consistently communicate safety expectations across North America.

"By partnering with ISN, we can more consistently manage compliance across various local and federal regulations while maintaining safety standards throughout North America," said PattyGoclowski, Director, Health, Safety & Environmental (HSE) at FirstLight. "Implementing ISNetworld at additional FirstLight sites in Canada helps enable staff to focus on activities on-site while ensuring contractors meet both regulatory and insurance requirements before arrival, upholding rigorous safety expectations."

According to a release, headquartered in Burlington, Massachusetts with offices in Montreal, Quebec, and Oshawa, Ontario, FirstLight operates renewable energy assets encompassing hydro, solar, and battery energy storage. ISN helps support FirstLight's goal of maintaining consistent safety standards through the use of ISNetworld tools and services. With the expansion to additional sites, the company will leverage ISNetworld's Online Training tool to help ensure contractors complete orientation requirements prior to arriving onsite and RAVS 360 to gain insights into contractors' HSE program implementation and safety culture, helping improve visibility into contractor performance, streamline onboarding, and align with evolving regulatory frameworks at local and federal levels.

"ISN is proud to support FirstLight as it continues to expand its renewable energy footprint in Canada," said Kim Ritchie, Senior Vice President of Canadian Operations at ISN. "By using ISNetworld to help increase consistency of contractor compliance practices, FirstLight exemplifies a proactive commitment to safety, operational efficiency, and environmental stewardship across its portfolio."

More information and complete details:

isn.com

firstlight.energy
