value.Title: April 28, 2026: Form Energy Scales Iron-Air Tech as Long-Duration Storage Goes Global

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHB-N4Y3-RWBY-P2KD-00000-00April 28, 2026: Form Energy Scales Iron-Air Tech as Long-Duration Storage Goes Global2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSApril 28, 2026: Form Energy Scales Iron-Air Tech as Long-Duration Storage Goes Global

On Apr. 29, 2026, Form Energy detailed its expansion plan, arguing that iron-air storage can complement lithium-ion by delivering long-duration reliability. The company notes that while lithium systems handle day-to-day variability well, they falter under multi-day outages, making up to 100 hours of continuous discharge meaningful for grid operators.

The pipeline has grown about 375% from roughly 200 MW / 20 GWh in Oct 2025 to 750 MW / 75 GWh by Apr 2026, driven by demand from utilities and technology partners. A notable contract with Google and Xcel Energy for 300 MW / 30 GWh, valued at about $1 billion, underscores confidence in the approach and its cost competitiveness.

The technology relies on iron, water, and air, reducing exposure to price volatility in minerals and easing supply chain risks. The West Virginia facility is expanding to meet future orders, targeting at least 500 MW of annual capacity by 2028.

Form Energy also flags international deployments, with its first project in Ireland designed to test performance amid high wind penetration and to demonstrate the viability of long-duration storage beyond the United States.

INDEX

SECTION 1 FORM ENERGY PROFILE

SECTION 2 FORM ENERGY TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 1 FORM ENERGY PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Form Energy is an American energy storage technology and manufacturing company that is developing and commercializing a pioneering iron-air battery capable of storing electricity for 100 hours at system costs competitive with legacy power plants. Form''s multi-day battery will reform the global electricity system to reliably run on 100% low-cost renewable energy, every day of the year.

value.Title: April 28, 2026: Iron-Air Battery Growth by Form Energy Signals Shift in Grid Reliability Solutions

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHH-MPX3-S7MH-62S5-00000-00April 28, 2026: Iron-Air Battery Growth by Form Energy Signals Shift in Grid Reliability Solutions2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSApril 28, 2026: Iron-Air Battery Growth by Form Energy Signals Shift in Grid Reliability Solutions

On Apr. 29, 2026, Form Energy outlined how its iron-air battery expansion could reshape long-duration storage and grid planning. The company emphasizes that lithium-ion systems, while effective for daily fluctuations, are limited when it comes to multi-day outages or prolonged periods of low renewable generation. Iron-air batteries can deliver up to 100 hours of discharge, a capability the company says is critical for grid resilience during severe weather and extended winter or summer lulls.

The expansion of the development pipeline is striking: from just over 200 MW / 20 GWh in October 2025 to roughly 750 MW / 75 GWh by April 2026, a 375% increase. The momentum is driven by partnerships, including a 300 MW / 30 GWh system with Google and Xcel Energy, a deal valued at around $1 billion that underscores the technology's scale and cost competitiveness.

The growth is underpinned by the use of iron, water, and air, which are abundant and less exposed to price swings tied to lithium and other minerals. The company's West Virginia manufacturing campus is being expanded to support a target capacity of at least 500 MW annually by 2028, aligning with customer demand and deployment timelines.

Form Energy's expansion beyond the United States is evident with its first international project in Ireland, designed to address high wind penetration and grid flexibility. The Ireland project illustrates the broader potential for long-duration storage as a global market curve for iron-air technology continues to ascend.

INDEX

SECTION 1 FORM ENERGY PROFILE

SECTION 2 FORM ENERGY TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 1 FORM ENERGY PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Form Energy is an American energy storage technology and manufacturing company that is developing and commercializing a pioneering iron-air battery capable of storing electricity for 100 hours at system costs competitive with legacy power plants. Form''s multi-day battery will reform the global electricity system to reliably run on 100% low-cost renewable energy, every day of the year.

urn:contentItem:6JHC-YV13-SHN4-40FX-00000-00April 28, 2026: Form Energy's Iron-Air Expansion Targets Multi-Day Grid Resilience and Cost Stability2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSApril 28, 2026: Form Energy's Iron-Air Expansion Targets Multi-Day Grid Resilience and Cost Stability

On Apr. 29, 2026, Form Energy outlined how its iron-air expansion could shift the economics of long-duration storage and strengthen grid reliability. The company notes that while lithium-ion remains strong for short runs, grids need a solution for multi-day outages and extended low-renewable windows, a niche iron-air is poised to fill with up to 100 hours of discharge.

The company highlighted a surge in the development pipeline — up 375% from 200 MW / 20 GWh in Oct 2025 to about 750 MW / 75 GWh by Apr 2026 — driven by partnerships including a 300 MW / 30 GWh project with Google and Xcel Energy, valued near $1 billion, illustrating a compelling cost structure for iron-air compared with established storage.

A core advantage is the use of iron, water, and air, which reduces exposure to price volatility in minerals such as lithium. The West Virginia plant is being expanded to achieve at least 500 MW of annual capacity by 2028 to support rising demand and deployments.

Internationally, Form Energy has Ireland in its sights, a project designed to test performance in high wind environments and to inform broader adoption. The company says Ireland could serve as a proving ground for long-duration storage and for refining manufacturing and deployment strategies.

value.Title: R.I. public media workers say they are unionizing
value.Overview: The Boston Globe, BUSINESS; Pg. D,3,999, 1104words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-6G93-SC56-B12M-00000-00R.I. public media workers say they are unionizing2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisBUSINESSPg. D,3,999R.I. public media workers say they are unionizingABSTRACT

Talking Points business briefs

BODY

MEDIA

R.I. public media workers unionizing

Employees at Ocean State Media, Rhode Island's joint NPR and PBS organization, announced Monday that they are forming a union with SAG-AFTRA. A majority of the organization's hosts, reporters, and producers that would be represented by the unit signed a petition to unionize, which was delivered to management Monday, according to a SAG-AFTRA press release. "We are proud to create public media every day for, and with, the people of Rhode Island and Southeastern Massachusetts," the Ocean State Media organizing committee said in a statement. "We believe that in order to sustainably and effectively serve that mission, the voices of staff need to be represented in our organization's decision-making." The unionization effort comes roughly two years after Rhode IslandPBS and The Public's Radio merged to form Ocean State Media. The organization named Pam Johnston, who previously served as the general manager of GBH News, as its first chief executive. — AIDAN RYAN

ENERGY

Data centers are driving carbon prices upward in Northeast

Data centers are driving a surge in carbon prices in the Northeast, helping push CO2 costs in the region past California and raising the prospect of higher energy costs for consumers. The price to emit a so-called short ton of CO2 into the atmosphere under the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative — a market covering 10 states, including all of New England — jumped 31 percent last week to $47.56. Traders are betting that Virginia's planned return to the market in July will boost demand for permits because the state is a major hub for data centers. The rally has sent prices past California's record CO2 price of $44 set in 2024. The US doesn't have a federal-level carbon price and instead has two state-run markets in California and Washington, the latter of which has a price around $66. That's in addition to the RGGI, which has existed since 2009 and is the country's oldest program. Carbon markets put a price on each ton of CO2 emitted into the atmosphere, typically by industry. The goal is to steadily reduce emissions while incentivizing the adoption of renewable energy and other clean technologies. RGGI covers only CO2 emissions from the power sector, which are expected to increase with the growing artificial intelligence-led demand for data centers. RGGI also risks adding to consumer bills, which are already rising because of the boom. — BLOOMBERG NEWS

ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE

Trump looks to vet new AI models

President Trump, who promoted a hands-off approach to artificial intelligence and gave Silicon Valley free rein to roll out the technology, is considering the introduction of government oversight over new AI models, according to US officials and people briefed on the deliberations. The administration is discussing an executive order to create an AI working group that would bring together tech executives and government officials to examine potential oversight procedures, according to US officials, who declined to be identified in order to discuss deliberations over sensitive policies. Among the potential plans is a formal government review process for new AI models. In meetings last week, White House officials told executives from Anthropic, Google, and OpenAI about some of those plans, people briefed on the conversations said. The working group is likely to consider a number of oversight approaches, officials said. But a review process could be similar to one being developed in Britain, which has assigned several government bodies to ensure that AI models meet certain safety standards, people in the tech industry and the administration said. — NEW YORK TIMES

FINANCIAL SERVICES

Anthropic, investment firms team up on AI integration

Anthropic is teaming up with several large investment firms to create a venture that will help companies integrate artificial intelligence tools into their systems, the latest example of the deepening ties between Wall Street and the AI industry. Private equity firms Blackstone and Hellman & Friedman and investment bank Goldman Sachs through its investment funds are among the financial backers in the new firm, which will work with companies to deploy Anthropic's AI model Claude. In announcing the creation of the firm on Monday, Anthropic and the investment firms said the technology around AI was changing so rapidly that many companies were finding it challenging to integrate Claude. The backers of the new firm said it would work with Anthropic's engineers to help companies deploy Claude, which has abilities that "change on a monthly or even weekly basis." — NEW YORK TIMES

FINANCE

N.Y. Fed president says rates will come down 'at some point'

Federal Reserve Bank of New York president John Williams said interest rates will need to come down "at some point" if inflation returns to the US central bank's 2 percent target, as he expects. "We will at some point need to be lowering interest rates to reflect the fact that inflation is lower," Williams told reporters Monday after a speech in New York City. "Inflation is higher this year than previously expected, so that pushes off a date of lowering interest rates, in my view, but it doesn't change that basic story." The Fed left its benchmark interest rate unchanged last week, though three officials dissented against the "easing bias" in the central bank's postmeeting statement, favoring language signaling the next rate move could be either a hike or a cut. Williams told reporters he was "very comfortable" with the current language in the statement because he doesn't "see anything in the day-to-day" suggesting there's an argument for a rate hike in the near term. — BLOOMBERG NEWS

TRADE

Canada to spend $1.1B to offset Trump's tariffs on metal

The government of Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney (right) unveiled measures worth $1.1 billion to help Canadian firms hurt by the Trump administration's changes to US tariffs on imports containing steel, aluminum, and copper. The change in early April put a 25 percent surcharge on the total value of products that contain these metals. Previously, the US applied a 50 percent tariff only to the actual metal content inside the products, but importers complained that regime was too complicated in practice. The change has been devastating to some Canadian businesses. Last month BRP Inc., which makes recreational vehicles such as Sea-Doo watercraft and Ski-Doo snowmobiles, withdrew its financial outlook for the 2027 fiscal year due to the tariff shift, which it expected would add $367 million in costs. —BLOOMBERG NEWs

value.Title: National Grid Expands DLR Technology Across 585km of UK Transmission Routes
value.Overview: News Bites - Western Europe: United Kingdom, ANNOUNCEMENTS, 812words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-5NH3-RWR1-N03W-00000-00National Grid Expands DLR Technology Across 585km of UK Transmission Routes2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSNational Grid Expands DLR Technology Across 585km of UK Transmission Routes

BRITISH DAILY STOCK REPORT

UNITED KINGDOM EDITION

05 May 2026 09:31 BST

National Grid PLC is set to expand its Dynamic Line Rating (DLR) technology to 585 kilometers of existing transmission routes across the UK. This initiative, announced on the 1st of May 2026, aims to deliver substantial savings for consumers, projected at up to £50 million in constraint costs over the next five years.

The DLR system utilizes advanced sensors to continuously monitor electricity transmission lines, calculating a dynamic line rating based on various factors, including wire specifications and weather conditions. This approach provides a more accurate assessment of the maximum power carrying capacity of transmission lines compared to traditional assumptions, which often underestimate their capabilities.

National Grid estimates that the implementation of DLR technology enables transmission lines to handle an average of 8% more electricity safely. The technology was initially tested in New York and Massachusetts in partnership with US-based provider LineVision before being introduced to the UK through a trial program in Cumbria that began in 2022. Following the success of the pilot, National Grid expanded the DLR system last year, adding an additional 275 kilometers of transmission routes.

The upcoming rollout will be conducted in partnership with LineVision, Belgian grid monitoring firm Ampacimon, and Danish cybersecurity company Heimdall. National Grid anticipates that the majority of the upgrades will be completed by 2028, with plans to incorporate drone technology for sensor installation on live power lines. Alice Delahunty, president of National Grid Electricity Transmission, emphasized that these innovations will not only enhance grid capacity but also facilitate the integration of renewable energy into the system.

The expansion of the grid's operational infrastructure is especially critical given the increasing challenges of grid capacity in the UK. Earlier this year, Foresight Solar Fund's investment manager, Toby Virno, highlighted the alarming rise in curtailment costs, which have reached £2.3 billion as the UK continues to commission more renewable energy capacity than its current grid can effectively accommodate. This DLR expansion by National Grid could be a pivotal move in addressing this pressing issue.

SECTION 1 NATIONAL GRID RECENT PRESS RELEASES

SECTION 2 NATIONAL GRID ACTIVITIES

SECTION 1 NATIONAL GRID RECENT PRESS RELEASES

November 16, 2025: National Grid announces dividend

National Grid today announced an interim dividend of GBX16.35 per share. The ex-dividend date is Thursday, November 20 and it is payable on Tuesday, January 13, 2026.

July 09, 2025: National Grid announces AGM

National Grid has announced its Annual General Meeting will take place on Wednesday, July 09.

May 18, 2025: National Grid announces dividend

National Grid today announced a final dividend of GBX30.88 per share. The ex-dividend date is Thursday, May 29, 2025 and it is payable on Thursday, July 17.

SECTION 2 NATIONAL GRID ACTIVITIES

Activities

National Grid PLC is a leading multinational utility company headquartered in the United Kingdom, with operations spanning the UK and northeastern United States. Its core business activities focus on the transmission and distribution of electricity and gas, ensuring reliable energy supply to over 20 million customers while maintaining critical infrastructure for national energy security. The company is actively investing in infrastructure to support the transition to a low-carbon economy, including grid modernization and renewable energy integration. Among its fastest-growing segments are offshore wind projects, which are expanding rapidly to meet rising demand for sustainable energy, as well as smart grid technologies and electric vehicle charging networks. These initiatives position National Grid as a key player in advancing energy efficiency and decarbonization, driving long-term growth through strategic investments and innovation. It is the largest Gas, water & multiutilities company by market capitalisation in the United Kingdom.

value.Title: Mastercard: How Technology Is Turning Sustainability Goals Into Measurable Action

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-2JF3-RRSG-W4HF-00000-00Mastercard: How Technology Is Turning Sustainability Goals Into Measurable Action2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisMastercard: How Technology Is Turning Sustainability Goals Into Measurable Action

From AI-driven insights to next-generation energy systems, innovation is turning sustainability from ambition into action.

NORTHAMPTON, MA / ACCESS Newswire / May 14, 2026 / By: Sarah Levitsky
Senior Specialist, Global Communications, Mastercard

For decades, environmental sustainability was treated as a long-term ambition. In 2026, that framing is changing. Across industries, technology is accelerating progress in ways that are tangible, measurable and increasingly embedded into the day-to-day operations of businesses.

What was once a fragmented effort, tracked in spreadsheets or siloed across teams, is becoming a connected, intelligent system. From corporate strategy to energy infrastructure, a new wave of innovation is redefining how businesses approach sustainability, not as a standalone initiative, but as a core driver of growth and efficiency.

The intelligence layer: From data to decision-making

At the center of this shift is data and the platforms that make sense of it. Companies are moving toward integrated sustainability systems that provide real-time visibility into everything from emissions to resource use. These platforms are increasingly acting as the digital backbone of sustainability strategies, translating complex data into clear, actionable insights, according to a recent story in Sustainability Magazine. For example, the supply chain visibility: A company can now vet thousands of suppliers simultaneously to ensure better sustainability in procurement.

Artificial intelligence is amplifying that impact. Already, many companies report using AI to support sustainability efforts, from analyzing environmental data to identifying inefficiencies and guiding strategic decisions a recent Deloitte survey shows. What once required manual reporting and retrospective analysis can now happen dynamically, enabling faster, more informed action across the business.

The result is a shift from tracking performance to actively shaping it, with technology acting as a constant feedback loop between insight and execution.

Powering the transition: Energy systems reimagined

Beyond corporate reporting, technology is also reshaping the physical systems that power the global economy. As electricity demand rises, driven in part by the rapid growth of AI and digital infrastructure, including cryptocurrency mining, climate tech is stepping in to meet the moment.

Investment is flowing into renewable energy, battery storage and grid modernization, all of which are critical to building a more flexible and resilient energy system, according to a new J.P. Morgan report. Next-generation batteries are improving how energy is stored and deployed, while modernized grids are better equipped to handle fluctuating supply and demand.

AI is also playing a growing role here, helping operators forecast demand, detect anomalies and optimize energy use in real time, the Deloitte survey says. Together, these technologies are not only supporting the transition to more renewable energy, but also making energy systems smarter, more adaptive and more efficient.

From strategy to operations

While large-scale infrastructure often dominates the conversation, some of the most meaningful progress is happening at the operational level. Across supply chains, workplaces and procurement systems, technology is enabling companies to embed sustainability into everyday decisions.

Advanced platforms are providing visibility into complex supplier networks, helping organizations better understand and manage their environmental impact across the value chain. At the same time, AI tools are simplifying how companies buy what they need and run their day-to-day operations, making it easier to be both efficient and more sustainable, according to Deloitte.

Even small changes, from reducing waste to optimizing resource use, are being scaled through technology, turning incremental improvements into measurable impact.

The next frontier: Innovation at scale

Beyond today's applications, a new generation of climate technologies is emerging to tackle some of the most complex sustainability challenges. Investment is accelerating in areas like critical minerals, which are essential to scaling clean energy technologies, as well as adaptation solutions designed to respond to the physical impacts of climate change, the J.P. Morgan report notes.

These innovations range from advanced materials and battery chemistries to sensor networks and AI-powered systems that can detect and respond to environmental risks in real time. At the same time, companies are exploring ways to go beyond reducing harm, using technology to support measurable, nature-positive outcomes.

Together, they point to a future where sustainability is not just about minimizing impact, but actively improving the systems that support it.

As these technologies continue to scale, the gap between intention and action is narrowing. Sustainability is becoming less about long-term ambition and more about real-time progress powered by technology that makes it possible to measure, manage and accelerate change at every level.

In that sense, the most important shift may not be any single innovation, but the growing realization that the tools to build a more sustainable future are already here and improving every day.

Continue reading here

Follow along Mastercard's journey to connect and power an inclusive, digital economy that benefits everyone, everywhere.

value.Title: Group Strengthens Board, Appoints Adebajo As Non-Executive Director

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJF-GJP3-RX11-53K0-00000-00Group Strengthens Board, Appoints Adebajo As Non-Executive Director2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisGroup Strengthens Board, Appoints Adebajo As Non-Executive Director

FCMB Group Plc has appointed Adepeju Adebajo as an independent non-executive director to its Board, following approval from the Central Bank of Nigeria.

The group said the appointment reflects its commitment to strengthening corporate governance and enhancing leadership capacity to support innovation, inclusivity and sustainable growth.

Adebajo brings over 30 years of experience spanning industry, renewable energy, agriculture, finance and consulting across Sub-Saharan Africa and Europe.

She is widely recognised for her expertise in business transformation, strategy development, operational efficiency and ESG implementation, having led organisations through major turnaround and growth initiatives.

Her professional background includes leadership roles as CEO of Cement at Lafarge Africa Plc, as well as chief executive positions at UTC Nigeria, Mouka Limited and Lumos Nigeria.

She also previously served as Assistant General Manager at United Bank for Africa Plc.

Earlier in her career, she worked with Boston Consulting Group, supporting clients in Europe, London and the United States on business transformation initiatives, and also worked with Citibank in London.

Adebajo founded Pinky Blue Limited, the exclusive franchisee of Mothercare in Nigeria, and served as Commissioner for Agriculture in Ogun State between 2017 and 2019.

She is also active in climate and sustainability advocacy, having founded the Climate Governance Initiative Nigeria and serving on the World Economic Forum Global Future Council on Climate and Nature Governance.

Her academic and executive training includes programmes at Harvard Business School, Imperial College London, INSEAD, the University of Cambridge and the Massachusetts Institute of Technology Sloan School of Management.

She currently chairs the Board of the Lagos State Employment Trust Fund and also serves on the boards of Ibstock Plc, Wolseley Limited and Husk Power Systems.

The Board of FCMB Group said her wealth of experience in transformation, strategy execution and sustainability will further strengthen the Groups leadership and support its long-term growth objectives in the Nigerian market.

value.Title: High schooler puts civics to action with environmental proposals
value.Overview: Andover Townsman (Massachusetts), STATE AND REGIONAL NEWS, 624words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK8-PVY3-S1TD-C1T5-00000-00High schooler puts civics to action with environmental proposals2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisSTATE AND REGIONAL NEWSHigh schooler puts civics to action with environmental proposalsAngelina BerubeAndover Townsman, Mass.

NORTH ANDOVER — Age knows no limits when it comes to civic engagement.

North Andover High School junior Mariah Eskel, 17, is proof of that as she has actively engaged in advocacy and policy development since the eighth grade and now awaits the next steps on moving two climate-focused bills through the state legislature.

A civics project on William Symmes Jr. bringing to light his role in the ratification of the U.S. Constitution initiated her passion for advocacy. Along with other students in her class, they identified a gap in public awareness in North Andover for the Massachusetts representative’s contributions to history.

Eskel said the experience introduced her and her classmates to the process of civic engagement and how local action can lead to meaningful change.

The experience spurred her to partner with former classmate Sofia FloresQuero Llamas to work on two proposals on climate policy. The two students researched environmental policy and how to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Both bills were filed in the Statehouse and referred to committees last year.

The high school student said she became interested in the subject matter after watching a lot of TV news.

“It’s important to engage in these issues and to make this world a better place because it’s hard to complain about it if you’re not trying to fix it,” Eskel said.

But she’s also built on what she learned from her middle-school civics teacher, Patrick McGravey.

Eskel said the civics class was the turning point for her where she realized if she wanted to make a change, she could. Her teacher was also the one who introduced her to Tarr and she’s collaborated with him for almost three years.

Eskel and FloresQuero Llamas drafted a proposal to incentivize renewable energy adoption for businesses and presented it to state Sen. Bruce Tarr, R-Gloucester, who worked with them on filing it in the Statehouse. The bill was proposed as a tax incentive for renewable energy investments and is before the Massachusetts Joint Committee on Revenue. It is scheduled to be heard on June 25 by the committee.

While they drafted that bill with Tarr, the students developed another proposal to advance research on grid battery storage technology for long-term energy sustainability.

She said Tarr challenged her and FloresQuero Llamas to research grid battery storage and how it could be more reliable. She said they determined research needed to be done between local universities and institutions on how to rely less on fossil fuels and how it all ties in with the renewable energy bill.

The proposal has moved onto the next committee after it was referred to the Massachusetts Joint Committee on Telecommunications, Utilities and Energy.

Eskel testified virtually last summer on the battery storage bill.

“The main goals were to encourage participation in clean energy, reduce our overall carbon emissions, and then make sustainable energy solutions more accessible statewide,” Eskel said.

The junior isn’t slowly down with her policy initiatives.

Eskel is working on an economic proposal focused on addressing inflation by improving economic efficiency and addressing high costs. She said Tarr plans to file the bill in January 2027 during the next legislative session.

“We made it so there were local manufacturing incentives, supporting domestic production and strengthening local economies,” Eskel said.

The junior said she finds economics interesting because it affects everyone.

“You go to the food store, the prices impact you,” she said. “You go to buy clothes, the prices impact you.

“I wanted to see how we could combat that and hopefully bring inflation down.”

value.Title: WEEKLY BUSINESS AFFAIRS SNAPSHOT: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-WBY3-S0GM-T0JB-00000-00WEEKLY BUSINESS AFFAIRS SNAPSHOT: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisWEEKLY BUSINESS AFFAIRS SNAPSHOT: RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC NEWS THIS PAST WEEK

+ RENAISSANCE CAPITAL LLC [3 Press Releases]

+ EMERGENCY MEDICAL SERVICES FIRM GMR SOLUTIONS SETS PRICE RANGE FOR $750 MILLION IPO

GMR Solutions, a leading provider of emergency medical services and alternate-site care in the United States, announced terms for its IPO. The Lewisville, Texas-based company plans to raise $750 million by offering 31.9 million shares at a price range of $22 to $25 per share. The details are drawn from the company's website.

+ ODYSSEY THERAPEUTICS PLANS ROUGHLY $232 MILLION IPO TO FUND AUTOIMMUNE PROGRAMS

Odyssey Therapeutics, a Phase 2 biotech developing precision therapies for autoimmune diseases, said it set terms for its IPO, planning to raise about $232 million by offering 13.2 million shares at $16 to $18. The Boston-based company focuses on targeted therapies for autoimmune and inflammatory diseases, leveraging immunobiology and drug discovery to identify key signaling pathways. Its lead programs include oral small-molecule candidates targeting RIPK2 and SLC15A4, with its most advanced drug showing early efficacy in ulcerative colitis and others in preclinical development. Odyssey aims to address inflammatory bowel disease and lupus, where treatment options remain limited. Source: Company Website

+ GEOTHERMAL DEVELOPER FERVO ENERGY ANNOUNCES $1.2 BILLION IPO TERMS WITH CORNERSTONE BACKING

Fervo Energy, a developer of next-generation geothermal energy projects, announced terms for its IPO on Monday. The Houston-based company aims to raise $1.2 billion by offering 55.6 million shares at a price range of $21 to $24. Cornerstone investors are expected to buy $350 million of shares, or 28% of the deal, according to the company website.

value.Title: Cadeler installs inaugural commissioned monopile at Hornsea 3
value.Overview: MarketLine NewsWire (Formerly Datamonitor), TECHNOLOGY & TELECOMMUNICATIONS; Energy & Utilities, 316words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-G0G3-S9TV-417X-00000-00Cadeler installs inaugural commissioned monopile at Hornsea 32026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisTECHNOLOGY & TELECOMMUNICATIONSEnergy & UtilitiesCadeler installs inaugural commissioned monopile at Hornsea 3

Upon completion, the wind farm is expected to supply renewable energy to more than 3.3 million UK homes.

Danish offshore wind installation company Cadeler has installed the first fully commissioned monopile foundation at Ørsted's 2.9GW Hornsea 3 offshore wind farm, located 120km off the Norfolk coast in the UK. This marks a significant milestone in developing one of the largest offshore wind projects globally. Upon completion, the wind farm is expected to supply renewable energy to more than 3.3 million homes in the UK.

Cadeler is managing the complete transport and installation (T&I) operations, covering a total of 197 offshore monopile foundations, at the Hornsea 3 offshore wind farm. The completion of this first monopile followed extensive planning and coordination among various teams, both onshore and offshore. Cadeler's A-class vessel, the Wind Ally, installed the monopile, while the Wind Orca managed the placement of secondary steel. Service operation vessel the ESVAGT FROUDE, operated by ESVAGT, performed the full commissioning, with post-installation work completed by Boston Energy. Cadeler will utilise three offshore wind installation vessels including the newly built Wind Ally throughout the Hornsea 3 project to handle the large-scale monopile foundations. Cadeler project director Bradley Scott said: "This milestone is not just about the first structure in the ground - it represents an important step as we continue expanding into full-scope foundation transport and installation. "Delivering projects at this scale requires close collaboration, detailed planning and strong execution across every phase of the operation. "We are proud to support Ørsted on Hornsea 3 and grateful to all teams offshore and onshore whose commitment and collaboration have contributed to reaching this point safely and successfully." In September last year, Cadeler signed a contract with Ocean Winds for the T&I of offshore wind turbines for the 390MW BC-Wind farm in the Polish Baltic Sea.

Its lead programs include oral small-molecule candidates targeting RIPK2 and SLC15A4, with its most advanced drug showing early efficacy in ulcerative colitis and others in preclinical development. Odyssey aims to address inflammatory bowel disease and lupus, where treatment options remain limited. Source: Company Website

+ GEOTHERMAL DEVELOPER FERVO ENERGY ANNOUNCES $1.2 BILLION IPO TERMS WITH CORNERSTONE BACKING

Fervo Energy, a developer of next-generation geothermal energy projects, announced terms for its IPO on Monday. The Houston-based company aims to raise $1.2 billion by offering 55.6 million shares at a price range of $21 to $24. Cornerstone investors are expected to buy $350 million of shares, or 28% of the deal, according to the company website.

value.Title: The German who worked on Saudi Arabia 's (failed) utopia has some advice for Spain

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-0FX3-RWY0-P31T-00000-00The German who worked on Saudi Arabia's (failed) utopia has some advice for Spain2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisThe German who worked on Saudi Arabia's (failed) utopia has some advice for Spain


The year Florian Lennert was born , humans first set foot on the Moon. Fifty-seven years later, space programs are aiming to establish permanent lunar bases where humans can live for extended periods. But while we dream of building 384,400 kilometers away, here on Earth we face serious housing problems. High prices and limited supply in overcrowded major cities, and other parts of the world suffocating in different ways: rising temperatures and desertification are redrawing the map of habitable zones on a planet facing water scarcity.


Lennert (1969, Berlin) has been dedicated to sustainable innovation for 30 years. This German professional boasts a background that includes positions at the European Commission and the London School of Economics (LSE), as well as collaborations with the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT). But what stands out most on his resume are the more than four years he spent as Head of Mobility at Neom, the futuristic project behind The Line-the 170-kilometer megalopolis enclosed within walls that was planned for the Saudi Arabian desert. One of those areas plagued by extreme heat and water stress that needs solutions, but where the Saudi government and foreign investors poured billions that ended up down the drain. Operational chaos, skyrocketing costs, and embezzlement have caused the project to collapse. In recent months, three of the major construction contracts that remained in place have been canceled. Saudi Arabia's attempts to salvage the project at all costs are now focused on the port of Neom, promoted as an alternative to connect the Gulf with Europe and Africa in light of the situation in the Strait of Hormuz due to the war between Iran, the U.S., and Israel. A last-ditch effort.
All of this is far from Lennert's mind. A year before Neom's official collapse, the expert embarked on other projects and set his sights on the Canary Islands, from where he advises startups on sustainable ocean technology and marine mobility. With his suitcase shuttling between Lanzarote and his hometown, where he is a researcher at the Helmholtz Research Institute for Sustainability, he speaks with us during his stop in Madrid, where he has delivered a series of lectures at the Ramón Areces Foundation on the ocean economy of the future. But before diving into the sea (for which he also has advice for Spain), there are concerns to address on land.
QUESTION. Given the news of recent months, do you think Neom has failed?
ANSWER. I think the project as a whole is misunderstood. The issue it sought to address was how to equip a desert region with infrastructure to obtain green energy and water. That part of the project continues. So does the port, which was intended to be a hub for exporting green hydrogen and a gateway for transporting goods across the Arabian Peninsula. The second part, with The Line, sought to create a corridor where different cities, built on virgin sites, could evolve over the course of 100 years. It wasn't just a building.
Q. But neither the project nor the plans for that 170-kilometer corridor have been realized, nor will they be. What was intended to be a massive development for 1.5 million residents along the Red Sea coast by 2030 has been scaled back to a projected population of 300,000 residents, at best, and vertical construction has come to a standstill...
A. What is clear is that some of the visions architects and other professionals had are neither economically viable nor ecologically desirable. It has been established that the height and architectural expression of that building conceived in the middle of the desert are not feasible. It will take more time, and it may be necessary to deploy different forms of cities to populate that area in the future.
Q. There are other futuristic cities being planned around the world, such as in Japan, Egypt, and Kazakhstan. What can Neom teach us?
A. We need to think about ways to build new cities; not in Europe, but certainly in China, India, and other countries. One lesson is that trying to build completely new cities from scratch-something that has been attempted several times in the past-is not easy. There is a high probability that you won't achieve the kind of organic city you need. The success stories are those where people have been able to introduce smart systems into existing cities. Vienna, Paris, and Singapore are demonstrating how this can be done while simultaneously becoming livable cities.
I also believe that The Line demonstrates how to design a corridor equipped with infrastructure, energy, water, and transportation. This planning allows smaller, distinct urban elements to evolve, preventing a kind of uncontrolled urban sprawl. That concept is valid and will continue. It also contributes to how to build an urban settlement in a world where, over the next 50 years, the population will continue to grow and the concentration of people in cities worldwide will keep increasing.
Q. In Spain, the housing problem is the main concern for citizens. How can this crisis be addressed?
A. This has become a major problem over the last 10 years, including in Berlin. It is partly driven by a concentration of wealth in city centers that drives out people with lower incomes. It is the flip side of intelligence and digitalization, which also fueled the growth of smart cities, because the supply of short-term and vacation rentals has been made possible by apps and digital systems, such as Airbnb and Booking. The use of downtown housing for tourism is a problem we must address through regulation, and Barcelona is leading the way in finding the right balance.
"We need public investment in public housing and regulations to control rents and guarantee tenants' rights."
The problem also stems from the fact that we used to have a lot of public housing, but over the past 20 or 30 years, many cities in Central Europe have lost it. Now private commercial interests control larger portions of the housing stock, and the pursuit of profit drives up rents and costs. Vienna is a city that has avoided this; there, 60% or 70% of the housing stock remains publicly owned, and people rent these units.
Q. As you said, Neom started by building new cities from scratch. This isn't a solution for Spain or for Europe, and we can't tear down our cities. But is there any lesson from the Neom project that can be applied to them?
A. I think the key is to combine strategies. Cities are living, breathing entities; it's not so easy to simply design a city based solely on engineering and the existing problems of others. But one clear idea is that if we want to solve the mobility problem in cities, we have to move people away from private cars and toward public transportation and shared mobility. Some cities, like Paris, have shown that it's possible to move in that direction and are having quite a bit of success. Barcelona, Madrid, and other Spanish cities have implemented a whole range of different systems for electric mobility, but I don't think we've managed to become that smart, digital, and sustainable city yet; we're perhaps at 50%. Urban planning and spatial planning are an important part of that, and they were at the heart of the mobility strategy in Neom.
Q. In other parts of the world, the housing problem will be linked to the extreme conditions resulting from climate change. What do we do with areas that are becoming uninhabitable?
A. The question is how we create social equity in a world where we face greater pressure on resources and where climate change is causing the degradation of urban environments. This often disproportionately affects low-income and poorer people much more significantly. If you look at the Global South, there are many countries suffering far more from global warming than we do, even though we are likely the cause. There are parts of Africa on the verge of becoming nearly uninhabitable, such as in sub-Saharan Africa, due to climate change. This poses an incredible threat to people's lives and livelihoods; at some point, they will need to find places to go and live. It is not a fair situation.
On the other hand, there are also economic disparities within European cities. Some of these are driven by the environment, others by the nature of capitalism, which tends to concentrate wealth in fewer and fewer hands. But I think we've been here before. We had a similar situation at the end of the 19th century, and it marked the beginning of the welfare state, basic social income, and the creation of public sector jobs... It was no longer viable to allow so many people to live in poverty. I believe that, inevitably, the only way to correct some of these injustices is perhaps to return to some form of more collective ownership and public management of resources.
The Canary Islands as an ocean technology hub
The Canary Islands are one of the regions in Europe most vulnerable to climate change, where they also face housing problems. That is why Lennert has chosen them to set up an accelerator that supports innovation in ocean renewable energy, aquaculture, and robotics. He says that their location, in the heart of a key oceanic region for Europe-near the Azores and Madeira and on the route to Africa and Cape Verde-is strategic from an ecological standpoint. But it is also strategic because it is a region where "every conceivable challenge converges, from tourism to migration, including energy supply and issues like desertification." Also, the problem now intrinsic to the sea: the oceans are bearing the brunt of global warming, absorbing 90% of the excess heat generated by greenhouse gases. The expert believes that the Canary Islands are the perfect place to seek solutions and protect them.
Q. What kind of sustainable ocean technology projects would you highlight?
A. We work, alongside partners such as the Canary Islands Ocean Platform (PLOCAN), with startups that want to test their solutions in the ocean. For example, cultivating algae to produce biofuels and bioplastics, like the startup Macrocarbon, or using wave energy to power desalination systems, as Wavepiston and Ocean's Oasis do. We're also working to build capacity to generate more wind and solar energy at sea and on floating systems. To test and scale these technologies, we connect startups and investors, but also the world of ocean science.
Q. You assert that this could be a goldmine for the Canary Islands, but also for Spain: you've analyzed that there is a market opportunity of 2.6 trillion euros for ocean technology. Why?
A. Spain is in a unique position, with two major oceans, two significant archipelagos, and very important ports. It is the largest employer in coastal tourism within the blue economy and has potential in key sectors such as maritime transport, renewable energy, and aquaculture. The question is how to become a leader in the blue economy in Europe and grow these industries, because some are new.
Q. In particular, you suggest investing in dual-use applications-both commercial and military. For example?
A. There are a number of technologies that have often been developed in the civilian sector and are now also finding applications in security and defense. Drones, autonomous underwater systems, sensors, satellite observation... For example, we can use ocean data to improve wind energy and better understand climate change, but it would also allow us to better monitor issues such as overfishing and illegal access to ocean resources.
Q. This serves as an example of how applications designed to protect the environment have an economic impact.
A. Absolutely. It is very important to understand that we need to protect the ocean because it already supports a large part of our economic activity. It produces food, provides us with transportation... 80% of the things you consume and use, like your smartphone, arrive here by ship. But it also performs a very important function: it generates much of the oxygen we breathe. And it stabilizes the climate.
So it's not a choice between the environment and the economy. The environment is essential for us to live and achieve economic prosperity in the future. We don't see it as a conflict; what we see is that there is now an opportunity to invest in technologies that help us protect it and also generate revenue.
"Spain has the geography and existing industries to be at the forefront of innovation in the ocean economy"
Q. Do you think Spain is still in time to join this sustainable economy, or are we too late?
A. I think it has great opportunities. There is a fantastic array of innovations and activities underway, whether in ports like Barcelona, Valencia, and Algeciras, where people are working on new ocean technologies; or in the Canary Islands, where truly interesting renewable energy applications are being tested. I believe Spain is in an excellent position. The question is whether it will be able to seize that opportunity and develop a national strategy focused on becoming a leader in sustainable blue innovation.
The year Florian Lennert was born , man first set foot on the Moon. Fifty-seven years later, space projects aim to create permanent lunar bases where humans can live for extended periods. But while we dream of building 384,400 kilometers away, here on Earth we face serious housing problems. High prices and limited supply in overcrowded major cities, and other parts of the world suffocating in different ways: rising temperatures and desertification are redrawing the map of habitable zones on a planet facing water scarcity.

value.Title: April 28, 2026: Form Energy Expands Iron-Air Battery, Aiming to Disrupt Long-Duration Grid Storage

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHD-M503-S6WK-11B8-00000-00April 28, 2026: Form Energy Expands Iron-Air Battery, Aiming to Disrupt Long-Duration Grid Storage2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:53:26ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSApril 28, 2026: Form Energy Expands Iron-Air Battery, Aiming to Disrupt Long-Duration Grid Storage

On Apr. 29, 2026, Form Energy outlined how its iron-air battery expansion could reshape long-duration storage and grid planning. The company argues that lithium-ion systems, while effective for daily fluctuations, are limited when it comes to multi-day outages or prolonged periods of low renewable generation. Iron-air batteries can deliver up to 100 hours of discharge, a capability the company says is critical for grid resilience during severe weather and extended winter or summer lulls.

As Form Energy scales, it reports a 375% expansion in its active development pipeline, from more than 200 MW / 20 GWh in Oct. 2025 to about 750 MW / 75 GWh by Apr. 2026. Much of the momentum comes from partnerships, including a 300 MW / 30 GWh system with Google and Xcel Energy, a deal valued at roughly $1 billion that highlights the technology's competitive cost structure.

A distinguishing feature is the use of iron, water, and air, which avoids volatile mineral supply chains tied to lithium. The West Virginia manufacturing site is being expanded to reach at least 500 MW of annual capacity by 2028 to meet rising demand. Form Energy also points to its first international project in Ireland, aimed at addressing high wind penetration and demonstrating the global applicability of long-duration storage.

INDEX

SECTION 1 FORM ENERGY PROFILE

SECTION 2 FORM ENERGY TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 1 FORM ENERGY PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Form Energy is an American energy storage technology and manufacturing company that is developing and commercializing a pioneering iron-air battery capable of storing electricity for 100 hours at system costs competitive with legacy power plants. Form''s multi-day battery will reform the global electricity system to reliably run on 100% low-cost renewable energy, every day of the year.

1.2 SUMMARY
